Federal University of Petroleum Resources Effurun opens Post-UTME application for 2026/2027 academic session
fullContent:
As the 2026 Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board examinations draw to a close, prospective students of the Federal University of Petroleum Resources Effurun are abuzz with excitement. In a verified announcement made available on the university's official website, https://fupre.edu.ng, the institution has opened its Post-UTME application for the 2026/2027 academic session. This development is a significant milestone for candidates who have been eagerly awaiting the opportunity to pursue their academic dreams at one of Nigeria's premier universities.

## Introduction to FUPRE
The Federal University of Petroleum Resources Effurun, established in 2007, has carved a niche for itself as a center of excellence in the fields of petroleum and gas engineering. With a strong commitment to academic excellence, research, and community service, the university has become a beacon of hope for young Nigerians seeking to acquire knowledge and skills in the oil and gas sector. As the university opens its doors to new intakes, prospective students are advised to take note of the admission requirements and procedures to avoid any hiccups in the application process.

## Official Policy Breakdown
According to the announcement, candidates who have chosen the Federal University of Petroleum Resources Effurun as their first choice institution in the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination are eligible to apply for the Post-UTME screening exercise. The university has also extended an invitation to candidates who have applied for Direct Entry admission to participate in the screening exercise. The application process, which commenced on May 15, 2026, is expected to run until December 31, 2026, or as may be determined by the university authorities.

## What Candidates Must Do Immediately
To participate in the Post-UTME screening exercise, candidates are required to visit the university's website, https://fupre.edu.ng, and follow the instructions outlined in the admission portal. The first step involves generating a remita retrieval reference number, which will be used to make a payment of two thousand naira only. Candidates are advised to use a valid email address and phone number, as these will be used to communicate with them throughout the application process. Upon payment, candidates will be required to fill out an online application form, which must be submitted before the deadline.

## Admission Requirements
To be eligible for admission into the Federal University of Petroleum Resources Effurun, candidates must have obtained a minimum of five credits in relevant subjects, including English Language and Mathematics, in not more than two sittings. Candidates who have applied for Direct Entry admission must possess a minimum of upper credit in their National Diploma or Higher National Diploma certificates. The university has also stipulated that candidates who have not chosen the Federal University of Petroleum Resources Effurun as their first choice institution in the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination must apply for a change of institution on the JAMB portal before proceeding with their Post-UTME application.

## Important Dates and Deadlines
As the application process is ongoing, candidates are advised to stay abreast of important dates and deadlines. The JAMB 2026 Change of Course portal, which opened on May 15, 2026, remains open until the end of the year, roughly December 31, 2026. Candidates who are yet to apply for the change of institution are advised to do so without delay to avoid any complications in their application process. The university has not announced a specific deadline for the Post-UTME application, but candidates are advised to submit their applications as soon as possible to avoid any last-minute rush.

## Quick Action Checklist
To ensure a seamless application process, candidates are advised to take the following steps:
* Visit the university's website, https://fupre.edu.ng, to access the admission portal
* Generate a remita retrieval reference number and make a payment of two thousand naira only
* Fill out the online application form and submit before the deadline
* Ensure that all required documents, including O'Level results and JAMB slip, are uploaded to the portal
* Apply for a change of institution on the JAMB portal, if necessary
* Stay abreast of important dates and deadlines, including the closure of the JAMB 2026 Change of Course portal on December 31, 2026
* Regularly check the university's website for updates on the admission process and screening exercise schedule.

The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has updated its e-facility portal instructions, advising 2026 UTME and DE candidates on safe authentication.
fullContent:
## JAMB Releases Fresh E-facility Portal Login Guidelines

In a bid to curb cyber threats and prevent unauthorized access to candidate profiles, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has released updated login instructions for its e-facility portal. The portal, which serves as the primary hub for checking admission status, printing UTME result slips, and purchasing PINs, is now protected by enhanced authentication measures.

Candidates are advised to use only official channels and personal computers or authorized CBT centers when accessing their profiles to protect their personal data.

## Resolving Common Profile Login Errors

Many candidates encounter errors such as "Invalid Email or Password" when trying to access the e-facility portal. JAMB has stated that this is usually due to incorrect registration details or typing mistakes during the login process. Candidates who have forgotten their passwords can utilize the official "Click here to reset" link on the portal page.

Furthermore, Direct Entry (DE) candidates seeking to purchase PINs must follow the validated secure gateway links to avoid purchasing invalid registration pins from unauthorized merchants.

## Direct Entry (DE) PIN and UTME Updates

Under the new guidelines, Direct Entry candidates must complete their physical verification profiles at accredited JAMB CBT offices before their profiles can be fully activated on the e-facility portal. This check is necessary to eliminate fraudulent academic credentials and maintain the integrity of direct admissions into 200-level university programs.

Candidates are reminded to ensure they do not disclose their secret login passwords or security questions to anyone, including cafe operators.

## Quick Action Checklist
- [ ] Access the official JAMB login portal via https://efacility.jamb.gov.ng.
- [ ] Ensure you have your active, registered email and password handy.
- [ ] If you experience login difficulties, click on the 'Forgot Password' link to reset.
- [ ] Purchase only verified 2026 UTME/DE registration PINs directly from the portal.
- [ ] Never share your login details with unauthorized persons.
- [ ] Check your CAPS status regularly to accept or reject admission offers.
title: JAMB Issues Fresh Directives on E-facility Login Procedures for 2026 DE and UTME Candidates

fullContent:
To safeguard the 2026 admission process, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has intensified its surveillance and monitoring of Computer-Based Test (CBT) centres across the country. According to the registrar, Prof. Is-haq Oloyede, the board has implemented compulsory, high-tech monitoring tools, including Microsoft and Digitech Live Cameras, to capture secondary images of candidates during registration. This measure is a direct response to irregularities detected during the 2025 registration cycle, aiming to eliminate identity theft and impersonation.

JAMB officials have stated that any centre whose activities cannot be remotely verified from the National Headquarters in Abuja will not receive payment for services, and their registrations will be immediately invalidated. This 'zero-tolerance' approach is intended to provide a level playing field for all applicants. The board is also collaborating with security agencies to monitor centres for potential collusion between staff and candidates.

For students, this means that the registration process is more secure but requires strict adherence to provided guidelines. Candidates are urged to register only at Board-approved centres, which can be verified on the official JAMB portal. Furthermore, students should avoid engaging in any activity that could be flagged as exam malpractice. As the 2026 session advances toward the post-UTME and physical screening phases, students are advised to keep their registration credentials secure and monitor their email for official communications regarding their admission status and departmental requirements.
title: JAMB Strengthens Monitoring of CBT Centres to Prevent Admission Fraud
excerpt: New technology and oversight measures are being deployed to ensure that 2026 UTME registration and processes remain free of manipulation.
An evaluation of the security measures and success rate of the 2026 Computer-Based Test conducted across Nigeria.
fullContent:
As the 2026 admission process progresses, officials from the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board have taken a moment to reflect on the successful conduct of the 2026 CBT examinations. Held across 989 centres throughout the nation, the examination faced several operational hurdles, including concerns regarding remote testing environments and security, yet the board maintained that the integrity of the process remained intact. The use of advanced biometric verification and real-time monitoring ensured that the results released in April and throughout May represent a fair assessment of candidate performance.

Despite the challenges that often accompany such a massive exercise, the board successfully managed the test for over 2.2 million candidates. The publication of initial results on the SMS platform (55019 and 66019) was a welcome initiative that allowed parents and students to access scores without relying on potentially fraudulent third-party websites. By consolidating the results and providing clear directives on admission scores, the board has minimized the confusion often felt by candidates during the post-examination phase.

Looking forward, the board intends to refine its testing methodology for 2027 to further reduce human intervention and streamline the delivery of results. For the current 2026 cohort, the focus now shifts entirely to institutional screening. Students are urged to maintain the same level of integrity they demonstrated during the CBT phase by avoiding 'admission racketeering' and 'shortcut services' that promise guaranteed admission for a fee. The official admission process is merit-based, and candidates who follow the guidelines provided by the JAMB portal are in the best position to secure their academic future.
title: Maintaining Integrity: JAMB's Successful 2026 CBT Implementation

excerpt: Following the conclusion of the 2026 UTME on April 16, JAMB has begun releasing results for the massive cohort of students who participated in the exam.
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially initiated the release of results for the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME). With over 2.2 million candidates participating in this year's exercise, the board has adopted a phased approach to result dissemination to ensure data integrity and security.

According to official statements, candidates can access their results by sending the text 'UTME RESULT' to the designated shortcodes 55019 or 66019 using the phone number utilized during the initial registration process. This automated system is designed to provide immediate feedback, bypassing the need for manual portals that often experience high traffic congestion. The examination, which concluded on April 16, 2026, saw the deployment of advanced surveillance technologies, including live camera monitoring from the JAMB headquarters in Abuja to ensure the integrity of the examination process.

Regarding underage candidates, the board has clarified that their results will be withheld until a complete evaluation process is finalized. Parents and guardians are encouraged to remain patient during this verification period. For those who encounter difficulties in accessing their results, the board recommends checking the official JAMB portal or visiting their zonal offices. As the admission season gains momentum, candidates are reminded that these results serve as the primary prerequisite for eligibility for the 2026/2027 academic session at all Nigerian tertiary institutions.
title: JAMB Commences Release of 2026 UTME Results for Over 2 Million Candidates

fullContent:
During the 2026 Policy Meeting held on May 11, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board announced a significant shift in its approach to admissions for critical sectors of the Nigerian economy. In an effort to encourage enrollment in Education and Agriculture-related programs, the board has introduced specific exemptions for candidates seeking these degrees. This policy is designed to bolster the number of students entering these vital fields, which have seen varying levels of interest in recent years.

While the UTME remains a standard requirement, the new guidelines provide a more flexible framework for institutional admission processes for these candidates. The initiative aims to address the shortage of qualified professionals in the agricultural and pedagogical sectors. By reducing barriers to entry, JAMB hopes to incentivize high-performing students to choose these developmental career paths.

Candidates intending to benefit from these special considerations must still ensure they meet the minimum entry requirements set by their specific universities. Prospective students are advised to check their respective school’s portal for details on how these exemptions are applied during their internal screening processes. Staying informed through official channels like the JAMB portal is essential for candidates to take advantage of this new policy shift for the 2026/2027 session.
title: New JAMB Policy Exempts Education and Agriculture Candidates from UTME Restrictions
excerpt: As part of the 2026 policy review, JAMB has introduced special considerations for candidates applying for Education and Agriculture courses.
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has extended the registration window by two weeks to accommodate candidates facing National Identification Number verification issues.
title: JAMB Announces Extension of 2026 UTME Registration Following Server Downtime
fullContent: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially announced a two-week extension for the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) registration. This decision follows widespread reports of technical difficulties associated with the National Identification Number (NIN) integration system. Professor Ishaq Oloyede, the Registrar, emphasized that no candidate should be disenfranchised due to system failures beyond their control. This extension has significant implications for admission chances. Historically, registration extensions correlate with an increase in total applicants, often pushing the final tally past the 2 million mark. For candidates, this means heightened competition. The extra time allows latecomers to enter the race, particularly those eyeing highly competitive courses like Medicine and Engineering where every fraction of a point in the UTME score matters. From an investigative standpoint, the server downtime reveals a bottleneck in the digital infrastructure of the country's identity management system. Candidates are advised to utilize this window to ensure their subject combinations are correct, as errors during this phase often lead to disqualification during the CAPS (Central Admissions Processing System) screening later in the year. The surge in applicants also suggests that the 'carry-over' effect from previous years remains high, putting pressure on the quota systems of federal universities. Admission seekers must now target scores above 280 to remain competitive in the merit category, as the pool of high-performing students is expected to expand with the extended preparation time. Furthermore, the extension might lead to a slight shift in the main examination timetable, potentially moving the start date from late April to early May. This provides a strategic advantage for students to intensify their revision, but it also increases the psychological pressure of a prolonged waiting period. Investigative data from previous cycles suggests that students who register during the extension period often face more challenges with center allocation, sometimes being posted to distant towns due to the exhaustion of capacity in urban hubs. Candidates are urged to finalize their biometric capturing immediately to avoid the last-minute rush that typically crashes the portal in the final 48 hours.

The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially announced a two-week extension for the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) registration period, which was originally scheduled to end in mid-February. This decision comes as a relief to thousands of candidates across Nigeria who have faced persistent difficulties in generating their unique profile codes. Reports from various geopolitical zones indicated that the 55019 and 66019 USSD codes, managed by major telecommunication providers, suffered from severe latency issues and intermittent downtime due to the unprecedented volume of concurrent requests. This extension is not merely a procedural delay but a strategic response to ensure that no candidate is disenfranchised by technical limitations beyond their control. For candidates, this development has significant implications for their admission chances. Historically, registration extensions lead to a surge in the total number of applicants, which naturally increases the competitiveness of the examination. With more students successfully registering, the 'cut-off' pressure for high-demand courses like Medicine, Law, and Nursing is expected to rise. Furthermore, the extension shifts the entire examination timeline, potentially pushing the main UTME dates closer to the rainy season, which often presents logistical challenges for candidates traveling to distant CBT centers. Analysis of previous cycles suggests that students who utilize this extra time for intensive revision rather than procrastination tend to perform better in the 'Use of English' section, which is often the decider in aggregate scores. However, the delay also means that the window between the UTME and Post-UTME screenings will be shorter, requiring students to begin their university-specific preparations concurrently with their JAMB studies. JAMB Registrar has warned that this is the final extension, advising parents to avoid the last-minute rush that often leads to data entry errors at Computer Based Test (CBT) centers. Candidates are encouraged to verify their O-level results upload on the CAPS portal during this period to avoid future disqualification.
title: JAMB Extends 2026 UTME Registration Deadline Amidst Profile Code Challenges
excerpt: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has announced a crucial two-week extension for the 2026 UTME registration following widespread USSD failures.

fullContent: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially entered the final phase of the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) registration, with the Registrar, Professor Ishaq Oloyede, emphasizing that the February 28 deadline is absolute. This year's exercise has been uniquely defined by the full integration of the National Identity Management Commission (NIMC) database, a move designed to eliminate identity theft but one that has created significant hurdles for thousands of applicants in the early weeks of February. Investigative reports from Computer-Based Test (CBT) centers in Lagos, Abuja, and Kano reveal that approximately 12 percent of candidates are facing 'record mismatch' errors where their NIMC data does not align with their JAMB profile. This technical bottleneck is more than a mere administrative hurdle; it directly affects admission chances by delaying the creation of the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS) profile. Without a verified profile by the end of February, candidates are ineligible for the Mock-UTME scheduled for late March, losing a vital opportunity to test their readiness against the 2026 exam software. Furthermore, JAMB has warned that any profile not fully synchronized by the February 28 cutoff will be flagged for manual review, which could delay results by several weeks. In the highly competitive landscape of Nigerian university admissions, a delayed result often means missing the first-batch merit list of top-tier institutions like the University of Ibadan or the University of Nigeria, Nsukka. These institutions typically fill 45 percent of their quotas from the merit list within weeks of the result release. For 2026 candidates, the message is clear: the final weeks of February are not just about payment, but about ensuring data integrity. Any discrepancy in name spelling or date of birth between the NIN and the JAMB portal will lead to automatic disqualification from the 2026/2027 admission cycle. Stakeholders advise candidates to visit NIMC enrollment centers for data correction before the final week of the month to avoid the predicted last-minute portal congestion that has historically crippled the registration system.
title: JAMB Final Registration Countdown: February 28 Deadline and the New NIMC Verification Protocol
excerpt: With the 2026 UTME registration window closing on February 28, JAMB has issued a final warning regarding the mandatory National Identity Management Commission synchronization and biometric integrity.
To promote inclusive education, JAMB has announced that the 2026 UTME registration forms will be free of charge for persons with disabilities, including those with Down Syndrome and Autism.
title: JAMB Unveils Free 2026 UTME Registration for Candidates with Special Needs
fullContent: In a landmark decision aimed at fostering equity in the Nigerian education sector, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) announced on February 2, 2026, that it will provide free registration forms for the 2026 UTME to candidates with various disabilities. This initiative is being spearheaded by the JAMB-Equal Opportunity Group (JEOG) and specifically targets individuals with Down Syndrome, Autism, Dyslexia, and Attention-Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), alongside those with visual and hearing impairments. The Registrar, Prof. Is-haq Oloyede, noted that this policy is not just about financial relief but about ensuring that 'no talent is lost due to physical or cognitive challenges.' This move significantly boosts the admission chances of a demographic that has historically been marginalized in the Nigerian tertiary system. By removing the financial hurdle of the N7,200 or N8,700 registration fee, JAMB expects a higher turnout of candidates from special needs schools. Beyond the free forms, the board is also introducing 'Audiobooks' for visually impaired candidates in this cycle, which will likely result in higher average scores for this group. Investigatively, this policy serves as a call to action for universities to upgrade their facilities. As more candidates with disabilities successfully pass the UTME through this initiative, institutions will face increased pressure to provide accessible lecture halls and inclusive learning materials. For candidates, this means they must correctly identify their disability status during the profile creation phase to benefit from the waiver. Admission officers in universities are also being briefed to prioritize these candidates during the screening process, provided they meet the minimum cut-off marks. This development marks a shift from a purely competitive admission model to one that actively considers social equity, potentially making 2026 a record-breaking year for inclusive enrollment in Nigeria’s federal universities.

As the 2026 admission cycle kicks into high gear, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has activated the window for institution changes. This process is critical for the over 2.2 million candidates who participated in this year's exercise and may wish to reconsider their choices based on their performance or the official cut-off marks released during the May policy meeting. The institution change window is a strategic period that allows students to align their university preferences with their actual UTME scores.

Before initiating a change of institution, candidates are strongly advised to exercise caution and conduct thorough research. It is imperative to check whether the new institution offers your desired course of study. Not every university or polytechnic maintains the same faculty structures, and a change made in haste could lead to disqualification if the institution does not run the selected program. Candidates should visit the official websites of their prospective schools to verify program accreditation and specific subject requirements.

Furthermore, the processing of admission through the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS) remains the definitive route for gaining entry. Once an institution change is confirmed, candidates must monitor their CAPS dashboard daily. Prompt acceptance of admission offers is essential, as delays can result in the loss of a student’s candidacy to others on the waiting list. The board emphasizes that while the change of institution window is an opportunity to correct errors, it should be treated with the seriousness required to avoid future administrative bottlenecks during registration at the university level.
title: JAMB UTME 2026 Institution Change: Essential Guidance for Candidates
excerpt: Candidates seeking to change their institutions following the 2026 UTME can now utilize the official JAMB portal to adjust their preferences.

fullContent:
As the 2026/2027 admission window officially opens, many candidates are evaluating their chances of securing a spot at their preferred institutions. A critical component of this process is the institution change and the use of the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS). With over 2.2 million candidates who sat for the 2026 UTME, competition for available spaces is at an all-time high.

Before initiating a change of institution, candidates must thoroughly investigate whether their target school offers their desired course of study. Not every university or polytechnic provides identical academic programs. Candidates are encouraged to verify this through the JAMB brochure and official university websites. Once a change is confirmed, monitoring the CAPS portal becomes the most important task. 

Admission offers are processed electronically, and candidates are required to accept or reject them promptly. Failing to check the portal regularly may result in missing out on a temporary offer that could expire. The admission cycle typically spans from June through September, though timelines can shift based on internal university calendars. The board urges candidates to remain vigilant, keep their login credentials secure, and avoid relying on third-party admission agents who promise 'guaranteed' slots, as all admissions must be processed through the legitimate JAMB portal.
title: Guide to Navigating 2026 UTME Institution Change and CAPS Management
excerpt: With the 2026/2027 admission cycle in full swing, students are encouraged to manage their institution choices via CAPS.
JAMB has activated the portal for institution changes, providing a critical window for candidates to adjust their school preferences for the 2026/2027 academic session.
title: JAMB UTME 2026 Institution Change: Essential Guidance for Candidates
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially activated the portal for institutional changes, allowing candidates who sat for the 2026 UTME to reconsider their preferred schools. This window is particularly crucial for candidates whose previous choices may no longer align with their performance or career goals. As the admission cycle progresses, candidates must ensure that their profile in the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS) is accurately updated to reflect their final desired institution.

Before initiating an institution change, candidates are strictly advised to conduct a thorough verification of their intended course requirements. Not every university or polytechnic offers the same programs, and prerequisites can vary significantly between schools. Candidates should cross-reference the JAMB brochure with the official academic calendars and websites of their target institutions to avoid choosing a school that does not offer their desired degree. Failure to verify this information can lead to lost admission opportunities.

Promptness is the key to securing a placement. As the 2026/2027 admission cycle is expected to run intensively from June through September, monitoring CAPS becomes the most important task for applicants. Candidates are encouraged to check their portals regularly for updates, as the period for accepting or rejecting admission offers is often limited. Proper engagement with the portal ensures that a candidate is positioned to receive and accept an offer from an institution that aligns with their academic aspirations.

excerpt: Heads of tertiary institutions have met with JAMB to align on the standards for the 2026/2027 admission exercise.
title: JAMB and Tertiary Institutions Harmonize Admission Standards for 2026
fullContent:
In a display of unity and dedication to academic excellence, the heads of various tertiary institutions across Nigeria met with JAMB officials to finalize the admission landscape for the upcoming academic session. This collaboration ensures that all universities, colleges of nursing, and polytechnics are operating from the same policy foundation, which was solidified during the 2026 Policy Meeting.

The agreement sets the minimum admissible scores at 150 for universities and nursing colleges, and 100 for polytechnics. This harmonization is vital for maintaining the integrity of the admissions process across the federation. By aligning these standards, JAMB aims to provide a clear and equitable path for students while empowering institutions to maintain their specific academic requirements.

Moving forward, the focus shifts toward the post-UTME screening phase, where institutions will assess candidates based on their O'Level performance, UTME scores, and other institutional criteria. Students are encouraged to stay informed by monitoring official university websites and the JAMB portal for updates regarding their specific choices of study.

The harmonious relationship between the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) and the heads of tertiary institutions remains a cornerstone of the Nigerian education system. As the 2026/2027 admission cycle commences, this partnership has been instrumental in setting the policies that govern the transition of millions of students. Recent meetings, involving key figures like the Registrar of JAMB, Prof. Is-haq Oloyede, and various academic leaders, have underscored a collective commitment to institutional integrity and student success.

During the recent policy meetings, stakeholders focused on harmonizing the internal admission requirements with the national UTME standards. This alignment is critical in preventing bottlenecks and ensuring that the admission process is equitable for all candidates, regardless of their preferred institution. By fostering this open dialogue, JAMB enables universities and polytechnics to set appropriate cut-off marks that reflect their specific departmental needs without compromising the overall quality of incoming students. The collaboration also highlights the importance of the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS), which continues to serve as the primary platform for monitoring and approving admission decisions.

Furthermore, the Board has continued to emphasize the role of technology in refining these processes. Through the digitisation of results and the implementation of strict verification procedures, the risk of fraud is significantly mitigated. As the academic session progresses, the Board and the participating institutions are dedicated to providing support services to candidates, ensuring that the journey from UTME application to final admission is smooth and efficient. Students are urged to remain focused on their academic preparations and to rely exclusively on official announcements from JAMB and their respective schools.
title: JAMB and Tertiary Institutions Harmonize Admission Processes for 2026
excerpt: Collaboration between JAMB and the heads of tertiary institutions is ensuring a seamless admission process for the 2026/2027 session.

title: Tertiary Institutions Harmonize 2026 Minimum Admission Scores
fullContent:
In a collaborative effort to maintain standard quality across the Nigerian tertiary education system, the heads of all tertiary institutions have formally agreed to a set of minimum admissible scores for the 2026 admission year. This resolution was finalized during the annual policy meeting, ensuring a unified approach that balances accessibility with academic excellence. The decision to fix the minimum scores at 150 for Universities and 100 for Polytechnics was made unanimously by the leadership of these institutions.

This harmonization is intended to provide a clear roadmap for both parents and prospective students. By establishing a definitive floor, institutions can better plan their intake capacity while candidates can realistically gauge their chances of securing admission into their preferred programs. Despite these minimums, many competitive institutions in the country are expected to set their own higher cut-off marks for specialized courses like Medicine, Engineering, and Law. Students are advised to verify the specific cut-off requirements for their intended school, as these may vary significantly based on institutional policy.

Institutional heads have also been tasked with ensuring that the admission processes are transparent, merit-based, and compliant with the Federal Character principle. The Ministry of Education has warned against the imposition of unauthorized levies on students during the screening process, emphasizing that the focus must remain on identifying candidates with the necessary aptitude for higher learning. As the admission cycle gains momentum, institutions are encouraged to communicate their individual screening timelines clearly via their official websites to prevent student confusion and maintain order during the post-UTME examination period.
excerpt: A formal agreement has been reached between JAMB and institutional heads regarding minimum admission thresholds.
Following a directive from the Council of Legal Education, eight Nigerian universities have been barred from admitting Law students for the 2025/2026 cycle.
fullContent: In a major shake-up affecting thousands of aspirants, JAMB has announced it will not process admissions for Law programs in eight Nigerian universities for the 2025/2026 academic session. The affected institutions include Kwara State University (KWASU), Redeemer’s University, Western Delta University, and several others. This decision stems from a suspension order by the Council of Legal Education (CLE) regarding violations of regulatory procedures and quota limits. For candidates who have already chosen these institutions as their first choice for Law, the implications are immediate and severe. Our analysis shows that this ban will trigger a massive migration of candidates during the 'Change of Course/Institution' window. Approximately 15,000 applicants are expected to compete for slots in 'safe' institutions like the University of Lagos (UNILAG), University of Ibadan (UI), and Ahmadu Bello University (ABU). This influx will inevitably drive up the 2026 Law cut-off marks in these top-tier universities to unprecedented levels, possibly exceeding a 300+ UTME aggregate in some cases. Admission chances for Law in 2026 are now heavily skewed toward those with exceptionally high scores who can withstand the increased competition at the remaining accredited faculties. Furthermore, JAMB's Head of Public Affairs, Fabian Benjamin, has advised affected students to immediately utilize the portal for institutional changes. Failure to do so will result in 'null admission' even if the university independently offers a slot. This situation highlights a growing trend of stricter regulatory oversight in Nigerian higher education, where professional bodies are increasingly willing to shut down non-compliant programs to maintain standards. Aspirants are urged to verify the current accreditation status of their chosen courses on the JAMB IBASS platform before finalizing their 2026 choices.
title: JAMB Bars Law Admissions in 8 Universities for 2026 Session

fullContent:
In a significant policy shift for the 2026 admission cycle, JAMB has clarified that while current university undergraduates are not prohibited from sitting for the UTME, they are now legally required to disclose their matriculation status during the registration process. This directive, explained by JAMB Public Communication Adviser Dr. Fabian Benjamin, is aimed at curbing the 'multiple admission' phenomenon that clogs the Central Admission Processing System (CAPS). In previous years, students would remain enrolled in one institution while securing a fresh admission elsewhere, effectively holding two spots and denying other qualified candidates a chance. 

This investigative report finds that JAMB's system has been upgraded with a cross-referencing algorithm that checks the 2026 applicant list against the national matriculation list of existing students. If a candidate is found to have concealed their current enrollment, they risk the 'Double Forfeiture' penalty: the immediate cancellation of their existing admission and the permanent withdrawal of the new 2026 offer. This policy is particularly critical for 100-level and 200-level students who seek to change courses or institutions by writing the UTME again rather than using the Inter-University Transfer window.

For these 'cross-over' candidates, the admission chances are now tied to their honesty and the timing of their withdrawal from their current institutions. Once a new admission is accepted on CAPS, the previous admission is legally and technically terminated in the database. This automated 'replacement' logic ensures that university quotas are managed more efficiently. Candidates are warned that 'self-styled' education consultants advising students to hide their status are misleading them into a trap, as the 2026 CAPS is more integrated with individual university registries than ever before. The takeaway for 2026 applicants is clear: transparency is the only way to safeguard your academic future.
title: New Disclosure Rule: JAMB Mandates Current University Students to Declare Status
excerpt: Undergraduates seeking to retake the UTME in 2026 must now formally disclose their existing matriculation status or face total forfeiture of all admissions.
Students already enrolled in tertiary institutions must now declare their status when registering for the 2026 UTME or face automatic forfeiture of all admissions.
fullContent: A new directive from the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has sent ripples through the Nigerian student community as the February 28 registration deadline approaches. The board has introduced a mandatory 'Disclosure of Matriculation Status' column in the 2026 registration portal. This policy targets students who are currently enrolled in a university or polytechnic but are seeking to 'cross-carpet' to a different course or institution by retaking the UTME. Under the new rule, failure to disclose an existing admission at the point of registration is considered an act of academic fraud. If a student is found to have concealed their current status, JAMB will automatically invalidate both their new registration and their existing admission, effectively leaving the student with no placement. This move is part of a broader strategy to eliminate 'multiple matriculations' and the practice of 'seat-hoarding,' where students occupy a spot in one institution while testing the waters for another. From an investigative standpoint, this policy is expected to significantly impact admission chances for fresh secondary school leavers. By forcing existing students to choose between staying in their current program or resigning to pursue a new one, JAMB is effectively reducing the 'hidden' competition in the UTME pool. Historically, a substantial percentage of high-scoring candidates were already undergraduates looking for 'better' courses; by making the stakes higher for these candidates, the board is ensuring that the available seats in top-tier universities are more accessible to first-time applicants. However, students who genuinely need to change courses due to poor fit or institutional issues now face a risky 'all-or-nothing' scenario. They must now weigh the certainty of their current degree against the uncertainty of a fresh admission cycle. JAMB spokesperson Dr. Fabian Benjamin emphasized that the board’s Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS) has been upgraded with AI to detect previous NIN-linked admissions, making concealment nearly impossible in the 2026 cycle.
title: JAMB Mandates Disclosure of Current Enrollment for 2026 UTME Candidates

fullContent: In a significant policy shift for the 2026 admission cycle, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has announced that students currently enrolled in any tertiary institution are ineligible to sit for the UTME unless they provide evidence of formal withdrawal. This move, aimed at curbing the 'placeholder' culture where students hold on to one admission while seeking a 'better' one, has sparked a heated debate among education stakeholders. The board clarified that once a candidate accepts an offer through the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS), they are legally recognized as a student of that institution. For such individuals to register for the 2026 UTME, they must now undergo a rigorous clearance process, which involves an official withdrawal letter and the deactivation of their profile on the CAPS portal. This investigative report finds that this policy is a direct response to the thousands of 'wasted' slots in premium courses like Medicine and Law, where students often gain admission but later abandon the seats for other opportunities without officially resigning. By enforcing this rule, JAMB intends to ensure that every admission slot is utilized by a committed student. However, the impact on admission chances is twofold. For new applicants, this is good news: it potentially frees up hundreds of seats that would have otherwise been locked by indecisive students. For 'returnee' candidates (those wanting to switch schools), the stakes have never been higher. The risk of withdrawing from a current program without a guarantee of success in the 2026 UTME is a gamble that many may not be willing to take. Education analysts suggest that this policy will likely lead to a slight dip in the total number of applicants but an increase in the 'quality' and 'intent' of the pool. Admission seekers are warned that the JAMB system is now integrated with the matriculation lists of all universities, making it nearly impossible to hide existing student status. Attempting to bypass this through multiple NINs or falsified records will lead to a permanent ban from tertiary education in Nigeria.
title: JAMB Enforces Mandatory Withdrawal Policy for Existing Students in 2026 UTME
excerpt: New regulations require students currently enrolled in tertiary institutions to formally withdraw before registering for the 2026 UTME.

title: JAMB Mandates Disclosure of Status for Undergraduates Seeking 2026 UTME Re-entry
fullContent: In a major policy clarification issued in late January and reinforced this February, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has addressed the growing trend of currently enrolled university students sitting for the UTME to switch courses or institutions. According to Dr. Fabian Benjamin, the board's Public Communication Adviser, JAMB does not prohibit matriculated students from taking the 2026 UTME, but it now mandates a formal disclosure of their current admission status during the registration process. This directive is designed to prevent the 'clogging' of the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS) where a single individual holds multiple active admission slots, thereby denying other qualified candidates a chance at tertiary education. For the 2026 cycle, any student who fails to disclose that they are already enrolled in a university, polytechnic, or college of education risks the immediate forfeiture of both their old and new admission if discovered. This policy is supported by the board's enhanced data integration with the National Identity Management Commission (NIMC) and individual university portals, making it easier to detect duplicate profiles. For many students, the desire to change from a less competitive course to a professional one like Medicine or Law often drives them to retake the JAMB exam. However, this new 'Mandatory Disclosure' rule adds a layer of risk; if a candidate is admitted into a new program but the board discovers a non-disclosed prior enrollment, they could be expelled from their current institution and barred from the new one. This ensures that the admission process remains transparent and that the limited available slots in high-demand departments are allocated to those who are not already occupying a space in the tertiary system. Candidates are urged to follow the 'Change of Admission' or 'Inter-University Transfer' protocols where possible, rather than bypassing the system through fresh UTME registration without disclosure.
excerpt: A new directive from JAMB requires students currently enrolled in tertiary institutions to declare their status when registering for the 2026 UTME to avoid forfeiture of all admissions.

fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has addressed reports regarding the eligibility of currently enrolled university students to retake the UTME. In a statement released in early February 2026, the Board's Public Communication Advisor, Dr. Fabian Benjamin, clarified that undergraduates are not barred from registering for the 2026 UTME or Direct Entry. However, the Board has introduced a 'Mandatory Disclosure' policy. Candidates who are already matriculated in any tertiary institution must declare their status during the 2026 registration. Failure to do so is now classified as an admission offense, punishable by the immediate forfeiture of both the current admission and any new admission offered through the 2026 cycle.

This policy is a strategic response to 'admission hoarding' and the rise of 'professional exam takers'—students who repeatedly sit for the UTME to help others or to occupy multiple slots across different universities. For a genuine student seeking a 'Change of Course' or 'Change of Institution,' this disclosure acts as a legal bridge. Once a new admission is secured and accepted via CAPS for the 2026/2027 session, the previous admission automatically ceases to exist. This prevents a single candidate from holding two matriculation numbers across different institutions, which has been a persistent data integrity issue for the Ministry of Education.

For the 2026 candidates, this rule tightens the admission loop. It means that the 'safety net' of holding onto an old admission while testing the waters with a new UTME score is effectively gone. Students must now be certain of their decision to switch, as the system is designed to detect prior matriculation records through the NIMC-JAMB integration. Investigative findings show that JAMB's automated system can now cross-reference 2026 registrations with the database of previous years' matriculation lists. Consequently, students who attempt to hide their current enrollment risk being blacklisted from the 2026/2027 admission exercise entirely, reducing their chances of a successful transition to a preferred course of study.
title: JAMB Issues Warning to Currently Enrolled Students Sitting 2026 UTME
excerpt: Matriculated students seeking to change institutions via the 2026 UTME must disclose their current status or risk losing both their old and new admission slots.
University students wishing to switch courses via the 2026 UTME must disclose their current matriculation status or face the forfeiture of both their old and new admissions.
title: JAMB Issues Stern Warning to Undergraduates Retaking 2026 UTME Without Status Disclosure
fullContent: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) issued a critical policy clarification on February 3, 2026, regarding 'double matriculation.' The board stated that while it is not an offense for a currently enrolled undergraduate to register for the 2026 UTME—often done to switch courses or institutions—it is a mandatory requirement to disclose their current matriculation status during registration. Dr. Fabian Benjamin, JAMB’s Public Communication Adviser, warned that failure to do so is a violation of the Board’s statutory mandate and will lead to the automatic cancellation of the new admission once detected. This warning is particularly relevant in February as many students in 100L or 200L are currently buying forms to escape 'unwanted' courses. The investigative depth of this issue reveals that JAMB’s Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS) has been upgraded with a cross-referencing algorithm that flags NINs already associated with active matriculation numbers in its database. This means the 'hidden switch' strategy used by many students is no longer viable. For these candidates, the disclosure rule is a double-edged sword: while it keeps them within the legal framework, it also alerts their current institution of their intent to leave. In terms of admission chances, candidates who disclose their status are treated fairly, but they must be prepared to formally withdraw from their current school before a new admission letter can be fully processed on CAPS. For those seeking highly competitive courses like Law or Medicine, any blemish on their registration record, such as a non-disclosure flag, provides a valid reason for universities to bypass them in favor of 'fresh' candidates. This policy emphasizes transparency and is part of a broader effort to accurately track student enrollment data across Nigeria, ensuring that admission slots are not 'wasted' by students who occupy seats in two different institutions simultaneously.

title: Official Announcement: Approved Minimum Admission Scores for 2026/2027
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially communicated the results of the 2026 Policy Meeting regarding the minimum tolerable admission scores for all tertiary institutions. This move is aimed at creating a standardized benchmark across the Nigerian educational landscape, ensuring that all institutions adhere to a unified grading and admission philosophy. According to the official directive, universities must maintain a minimum score of 150 for admission consideration. Similarly, Colleges of Nursing are expected to adhere to a score of 140, while Polytechnics are set at a minimum of 100.

These scores represent the absolute floor for institutional admission. It is important to note that individual universities reserve the right to set higher departmental cut-off marks depending on the number of applicants for specific programs. For example, highly competitive courses like Medicine, Law, and Engineering may require significantly higher scores than the national minimum. Students are encouraged to familiarize themselves with the specific requirements of their chosen institutions as listed on the JAMB website.

In addition to the scores, the board has cautioned against the proliferation of illegal admission processes. All admissions must be routed through the JAMB portal to be valid. Students who bypass the official channel are not recognized as legitimate students by the board, which could lead to complications with the National Youth Service Corps (NYSC) registration in the future. The board remains committed to a merit-based, transparent, and efficient admission cycle for all candidates across the 989 centres where the CBT examinations were successfully conducted earlier this year.
excerpt: JAMB has officially published the approved minimum scores for various tertiary institutions in Nigeria for the current session.

fullContent: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has finalized the date for the 2026 optional Mock-UTME. According to the official timetable, the mock examination will hold on Saturday, March 28, 2026. Candidates who wish to participate are reminded that the deadline for selecting the mock option during registration is Monday, February 16, 2026. The mock exam is designed to familiarize candidates with the CBT environment ahead of the main UTME in April. JAMB also clarified that the official novel for the 2026 exercise is 'The Lekki Headmaster' by Kabir Alabi Garba, which will be the basis for English Language questions.
title: JAMB Fixes March 28 for 2026 Mock-UTME, Sets Deadline for Selection
excerpt: Candidates participating in the 2026 optional Mock-UTME must conclude their selection by February 16, with the exam scheduled for March 28.

fullContent: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has finalized arrangements for the 2026 optional Mock UTME, scheduled to hold on March 28, 2026. This preparatory exam is a critical milestone for candidates looking to familiarize themselves with the Computer Based Test (CBT) environment before the main UTME in April. In conjunction with this announcement, JAMB has officially distributed the mandatory reading text for the 2026 cycle: 'The Lekki Headmaster' by Kabir Alabi Garba. Every candidate is entitled to a copy of this novel upon successful registration. The choice of 'The Lekki Headmaster' follows JAMB's tradition of selecting literature that addresses contemporary Nigerian social issues, ethics, and the value of education. Candidates are reminded that questions from this novel are compulsory and appear in the Use of English section for all candidates, regardless of their chosen course of study. Participation in the mock exam, while optional, has a high correlation with success in the main UTME. Investigative data suggests that students who participate in the mock tend to score 15 to 20 percent higher in the main exam due to reduced anxiety and better time management skills. The mock exam serves as a diagnostic tool, allowing candidates to identify their weak points in subjects like Physics, Mathematics, and Literature before the high-stakes main examination window between April 16 and April 25. For the 2026 session, JAMB has upgraded the CBT software to include better navigation features and enhanced security against 'screen-sharing' malpractices. Candidates who did not register for the mock are still encouraged to study the novel extensively, as the Board often uses the Mock UTME to test the difficulty level of questions derived from the new literature text. The N1,500 service charge for the mock exam is payable directly to the CBT center on the day of the exam. As February progresses, schools and tutorial centers are pivoting their curriculum to focus on the themes and characters of 'The Lekki Headmaster,' ensuring that students are not caught off guard by the deep comprehension and analytical questions typical of JAMB's assessment style. Failure to read the prescribed text is often the difference between a 200 and 250 score, making it a high-priority item for any serious aspirant.
title: JAMB Announces March 28 for 2026 Mock UTME; Introduces New Literature Text 'The Lekki Headmaster'
excerpt: Candidates who opted for the optional mock exam now have a confirmed date of March 28, 2026, as JAMB releases the new mandatory reading text for the year.

fullContent: Preparation for the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) has reached a critical milestone with the announcement that the selection window for the optional Mock-UTME will close on February 16, 2026. The mock examination, scheduled for late March, is designed to give candidates a feel of the actual exam environment while allowing JAMB to stress-test its new CBT infrastructure. For the 2026 cycle, JAMB has introduced an upgraded testing interface that includes enhanced video proctoring and a revised calculator layout. Participating in the mock exam has historically been a strong predictor of success in the main UTME. Data from previous years shows that candidates who sit for the mock tend to score 15-20% higher than those who do not, primarily due to reduced 'computer anxiety' and better time management. In a competitive year like 2026, where admission slots are limited, this advantage could be the difference between meeting the cut-off for a top-tier university and being redirected to a less preferred institution. The board has also cautioned that the mock exam will be used to identify potential technical blind spots in rural CBT centers. Candidates in these areas are particularly encouraged to participate to ensure their centers are fully operational before the high-stakes April exams. Furthermore, the results of the mock exam will provide an early benchmark for candidates to gauge their readiness. If a student scores poorly in the mock, the remaining weeks in February and March provide a final opportunity to intensive their studies. Admission chances are not directly affected by mock scores—as the results are not used for placement—but the psychological and technical preparation it provides is invaluable. With the deadline for opting-in fast approaching on February 16, candidates who have already registered for the main UTME are urged to log back into their profiles and confirm their participation to avoid missing this critical preparatory exercise.
title: JAMB Mock-UTME 2026: Selection Window Closes February 16 as Board Tests New CBT Infrastructure
excerpt: Candidates have until February 16 to opt-in for the optional Mock-UTME, which will feature upgraded CBT software and enhanced security protocols.

title: Technology Deployment: JAMB Monitors 2026 Centers via Live Surveillance
fullContent:
For the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME), the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) successfully implemented a high-tech monitoring system. This system allowed the Board to oversee activities at all 989 approved Computer-Based Test (CBT) centers directly from the JAMB Headquarters in Abuja. By utilizing advanced Microsoft and Digitech live camera technologies, the board aimed to eliminate malpractice and maintain the credibility of the certification process.

Professor Oloyede, in an earlier briefing, highlighted that the deployment of this technology was a major factor in the smooth conduct of the exam. The surveillance covered not only the examination hall but also the registration and verification areas, ensuring that candidates were who they claimed to be. This level of oversight has become a cornerstone of the board's effort to standardize testing across the nation's diverse geographical regions.

The use of these technologies is part of a multi-year strategy to digitize the entire admission experience. For the 2026 session, the focus was on ensuring that even centers in remote locations were brought up to the necessary technological standard. Students reported a generally positive experience with the testing equipment, suggesting that the board’s emphasis on center infrastructure is yielding results. The success of this monitoring initiative provides a blueprint for future examinations, potentially setting the stage for more advanced remote testing capabilities in the coming years.
excerpt: To maintain integrity in the 2026 UTME, JAMB utilized live camera surveillance across all accredited Computer-Based Test (CBT) centers.

fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board, under the leadership of Professor Oloyede, successfully deployed high-tech live surveillance systems to monitor all CBT registration and examination centers throughout the 2026 UTME period. By utilizing Microsoft and Digitech-powered live camera feeds, the board was able to monitor operations in real-time from its headquarters in Abuja.

This investment in technology is a cornerstone of the board's commitment to eliminating malpractice and ensuring that the examination process remains transparent and equitable. Throughout the 2026 exercise, officials monitored the centers for any signs of technical hitches or human interference. The success of this monitoring strategy has contributed significantly to the prompt release of results and the overall integrity of the 2026 exam cycle.

These measures serve as a deterrent to exam fraudsters and ensure that students are provided with the best possible service across all 989 centers. The board continues to upgrade its technical infrastructure to match global standards, providing a secure platform for millions of Nigerian students to pursue their higher education goals.
title: JAMB Deploys Advanced Live-Monitoring Cameras for 2026 UTME
excerpt: JAMB has utilized cutting-edge surveillance technology to monitor registration and examination centers across Nigeria.
JAMB has scheduled a mop-up examination for candidates who experienced biometric verification challenges during the main UTME.
title: JAMB Announces Mop-Up Examination Date for 2026 UTME
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially announced that the 2026 UTME mop-up examination will take place on Saturday, June 13, 2026. This special exercise is designed exclusively for candidates who were verified biometrically at their centers but were unable to complete their examination due to technical malfunctions or center-specific irregularities that occurred during the main testing window.

Candidates falling into this category have been urged to confirm their eligibility via the JAMB portal. The board clarified that this opportunity is not open to candidates who missed their scheduled sessions due to lateness or failure to follow instructions. Applicants are expected to print their notification slips starting from one week before the scheduled date to confirm their specific center location and time. The board remains committed to ensuring that no qualified candidate is disenfranchised due to technical errors beyond their control.

Participants are advised to arrive early at their designated centers and follow all safety and security protocols. Biometric verification will once again be strictly enforced at the entry point of every examination center. JAMB reiterated that all standard regulations regarding the use of prohibited items, including mobile phones and electronic devices, will remain in effect. Failure to comply with these rules will lead to immediate disqualification. Candidates should keep track of the official JAMB social media channels and the official website for any further updates or necessary adjustments to the examination schedule.

excerpt: Candidates who experienced technical difficulties during the main UTME have been scheduled for a mop-up examination on June 13, 2026.
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has officially scheduled the 2026 UTME Mop-up Examination for June 13, 2026. This exercise is strictly reserved for candidates who faced verifiable technical issues during the main examination conducted earlier this year. The Board has spent the last few weeks reviewing reports from various CBT centres across the country to identify those impacted by glitches, power failures, or system errors.

Candidates eligible for this mop-up examination will be notified via their registered email addresses and phone numbers. It is expected that the affected students will also be able to print their new examination notification slips from the JAMB portal at least 48 hours before the exam date. The board has assured all stakeholders that the mop-up exam will follow the same rigorous security protocols as the main UTME to ensure the integrity of the results.

For those who are not selected for the mop-up, JAMB notes that their initial status stands, and they are encouraged to proceed with the regular admission application process. The Board advises parents and candidates to refrain from spreading unverified information on social media. Any official updates regarding the list of affected centres or candidate names will only be published through the official JAMB website or their verified communication channels.
title: JAMB Confirms UTME Mop-up Examination Date for June 2026
JAMB Fixes June 13 For 2026 UTME Mop-Up Examination
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially announced Saturday, June 13, 2026, as the date for the conduct of the UTME mop-up examination. This special exercise is exclusively for candidates who, despite being biometrically verified during the main examination window (April 16 to April 29), were unable to complete their tests due to unforeseen technical disruptions or administrative challenges.

According to the statement released by the Public Communication Advisor, Fabian Benjamin, the mop-up exam represents the final opportunity for these affected students to secure their results for the 2026/2027 admission exercise. Candidates are advised to check their JAMB profiles via the official website to verify if they have been shortlisted for this mop-up exercise. Attendance is mandatory for those whose initial results were withheld or cancelled due to technical faults at specific centres.

Candidates are warned against complacency and must ensure they arrive at their designated centres well ahead of time. Valid identification documents, including the 2026 UTME notification slip, will be required at the venue. JAMB reiterated that this process is free for eligible candidates and warned against any form of payment to third parties for 'special centers' or 'exam assistance.'
excerpt: JAMB has officially scheduled June 13, 2026, as the final mop-up date for candidates who faced technical issues during the main examination.
JAMB has released the results of the 2026 UTME mop-up examination and started setting performance metrics for university admission processes.
fullContent: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially released the results of the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) mop-up exams, which were organized for candidates who missed the main test due to verified technical issues or biometric discrepancies. Candidates can now access their scores via the online e-facility portal.\n\nWith the release of these final scores, JAMB announced it has commenced the processing of national admission rankings. This central database system organizes candidate performance statistics, allowing tertiary institutions across the nation to evaluate the competitive scores of prospective applicants ahead of their respective Post-UTME screening exercises.
title: JAMB Releases 2026 UTME Mop-Up Results, Initiates Institutional Ranking Process
JAMB Maintains Strict NIMC Data Integration Policy for 2026 Admissions
fullContent:
In a bid to curb identity fraud and enhance the integrity of the admission process, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has reiterated its commitment to the compulsory integration of National Identity Management Commission (NIMC) data for all 2026 UTME candidates. Registrar Prof. Is-haq Oloyede stressed that the board will not process, correct, or accept any personal data that does not correspond with the information generated by the NIMC.

This policy is part of a broader strategy to eliminate 'ghost' candidates and reduce manipulation during the registration and examination phases. The board clarified that any attempt to bypass these verification protocols during registration or the post-UTME phase will result in the immediate invalidation of the candidate's application. The requirement includes the use of advanced biometric verification, including mandatory high-definition camera capturing for the second image during registration.

Candidates are warned that the board has invested in sophisticated monitoring tools to ensure that registration activities at various centres across the country can be viewed in real-time from the JAMB National Headquarters in Abuja. Any centre failing to comply with these technological requirements faces decertification and potential legal action. Parents and students are advised to ensure their NIN records are up-to-date and correctly matched with their JAMB profile to avoid unnecessary complications during the admission screening exercises which are slated to begin later in the year.
excerpt: JAMB reiterates that all candidate data for the 2026 session must align with NIMC records to avoid disqualification.

title: JAMB Implements Mandatory 'TEMPL007' Re-Query for NIMC Data Discrepancies
fullContent: As the 2026 UTME registration enters its final week, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has introduced a critical technical requirement for candidates who have made changes to their National Identification Number (NIN) profile. The new 'TEMPL007' Re-Query mandate requires any candidate whose data—such as name spelling, date of birth, or state of origin—has been modified at the National Identity Management Commission (NIMC) to manually synchronize these changes with the JAMB database at an accredited center. This process is crucial because JAMB's Central Admission Processing System (CAPS) will, for the 2026 cycle, automatically flag and disqualify any candidate whose registration data does not perfectly align with their verified NIMC records. This technical hurdle has severe implications for admission chances. Many candidates often discover too late that a minor mismatch between their JAMB profile and their O-Level results or NIN data prevents their admission from being 'recommended' by the university. In previous years, these mismatches could sometimes be resolved through a lengthy manual correction process after admission offers were made. However, for 2026, JAMB has integrated a real-time 'Validation Lock.' If the TEMPL007 re-query is not performed during the registration window, the candidate's profile remains 'unreconciled,' and CAPS will simply skip over such profiles during the automated selection phases, regardless of how high the candidate's score is. This means a candidate with 340 in UTME could lose their admission spot to someone with 250 simply because the latter's data was verified and synchronized. To navigate this, candidates must visit an accredited CBT center, pay the prescribed synchronization fee, and ensure they receive a 'Data Re-Query Success' slip. This technical diligence is now as important as the exam itself, as it forms the bedrock of a candidate's eligibility within the increasingly automated admission ecosystem of 2026.
excerpt: Candidates with updated NIN records must now use the TEMPL007 process to synchronize their data with JAMB or face automatic admission disqualification.
JAMB Registrar Professor Ishaq Oloyede mandates that all 924 accredited CBT centers must be remotely visible from Abuja or face immediate de-accreditation.
title: No Vision, No Registration: JAMB Deploys AI-Driven Live Monitoring for 2026 CBT Centers
fullContent: In a bold move to eliminate examination malpractice at the source, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has introduced the 'No Vision, No Registration, No UTME' policy for the 2026 cycle. This directive, finalized in mid-February, requires all 924 screened Computer-Based Test (CBT) centers to maintain a constant, unhindered live video feed to the JAMB National Headquarters in Abuja. Investigative reports from centers across Lagos, Kano, and Enugu show that centers failing to provide this digital transparency have already been barred from the 2026 registration exercise, which ends on February 28. This policy is an escalation of the Board's war against 'miracle centers'—locations where candidates are often assisted in exchange for illegal fees. By utilizing AI-driven proctoring software that flags suspicious movement or unauthorized personnel in the registration hall, JAMB is effectively decentralizing its monitoring while centralizing its enforcement. For candidates, this policy directly impacts admission chances by restoring the integrity of the 'cut-off mark.' In previous years, score inflation caused by malpractice at compromised centers forced top-tier universities like the University of Ibadan and Obafemi Awolowo University to raise their internal thresholds to unsustainable levels. With the 2026 security measures, there is a projection that 'genuine' high scores will carry more weight, as the pool of candidates with artificially inflated results is expected to shrink by as much as 25 percent. Furthermore, the integration of National Identification Number (NIN) data with live facial recognition during registration ensures that the person who registers is the same person who sits for the exam in April. This technology eliminates the 'proxy candidate' syndrome that has long plagued Nigerian tertiary admissions. While some center owners complain about the high cost of the required high-speed fiber-optic connections and 360-degree cameras, JAMB remains firm, stating that any center without 'vision' is a center with something to hide. Candidates are advised to verify that their chosen center is fully compliant before concluding registration, as data from a de-accredited center may be invalidated, requiring a costly and stressful re-registration process at a legal site.

title: JAMB Implements 'No Vision, No Registration' and Mandatory Status Disclosure for 2026
fullContent: The 2026 UTME registration period has introduced two stringent integrity measures: the 'No Vision, No Registration' policy and the 'Mandatory Status Disclosure.' The 'No Vision' policy stipulates that any CBT center whose activities cannot be monitored in real-time by JAMB's headquarters via remote surveillance will be barred from registering candidates. This is a direct strike against registration infractions such as the use of proxy biometrics and the creation of 'ghost' candidates. Simultaneously, JAMB has issued a directive requiring all candidates who are already enrolled in a tertiary institution—but are seeking a fresh admission—to disclose their current matriculation status during registration. Investigative reports suggest this is aimed at curbing 'admission hoarding,' where students occupy multiple slots across different universities, thereby denying others a chance. Prof. Fabian Benjamin, JAMB's spokesperson, clarified that the board does not prohibit current students from seeking a change of institution through the UTME, but failure to declare their existing status will lead to the immediate forfeiture of both the old and new admissions. This crackdown significantly levels the playing field for first-time applicants. By flushing out 'professional exam takers' and dual-admission seekers, the 'true' competition pool becomes more transparent. For the average 2026 candidate, this means that the scores reflecting on the merit lists are more likely to be legitimate, potentially lowering the unofficial 'inflated' cut-off marks that occur when high-scoring proxies dominate the top percentiles. Furthermore, the real-time monitoring of centers ensures that candidates are not extorted for fees beyond the official ₦7,200 (without mock) or ₦8,700 (with mock) packages. As February progresses, JAMB's technical teams are conducting random 'integrity sweeps' of centers. Candidates are warned that any attempt to bypass the biometric verification through 'special centers' will result in a permanent ban from all future JAMB-conducted examinations. These measures, while strict, are designed to restore confidence in the Nigerian university entry system.
excerpt: New integrity measures require current university students to declare their status and mandate remote monitoring for all 2026 UTME registration centers.

fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has issued a clarification on the cut-off marks for admission, following some confusion in the public domain. For the 2026/2027 academic session, the minimum admissible score for universities is set at 150. Crucially, the board has also placed Colleges of Nursing at the same threshold of 150, reflecting the rigorous standards required for health-related academic disciplines. Meanwhile, polytechnics remain at a 100-point threshold.

This clarification is vital for thousands of students aspiring to enter the nursing profession, which remains one of the most competitive fields in the Nigerian higher education sector. By setting the cut-off at 150, the board ensures that nursing programs maintain a quality baseline. Candidates are advised that while 150 is the national minimum, many prestigious nursing schools and colleges often require a significantly higher score for admission, given the volume of high-scoring applicants they receive each year.

Students should not mistake the national minimum for the actual admission score of their target school. It is imperative that all candidates research the historical and current cut-off averages for their desired institutions. Monitoring the official websites of these nursing schools is just as important as checking the JAMB portal. Candidates are encouraged to maintain a proactive stance in their admission search, ensuring they are prepared for the competitive selection process that will take place in the coming months.
title: JAMB Clarifies Cut-off Marks for Nursing and Tertiary Institutions
excerpt: JAMB has provided specific clarification regarding the cut-off marks for Colleges of Nursing in the 2026/2027 academic session.
Official summary of the approved minimum admission scores across Nigerian tertiary institutions for the 2026 session.
title: 2026 Admission Policy Guidelines Approved by JAMB and Stakeholders
fullContent:
Following the 2026 Policy Meeting, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has published the official minimum tolerable admission scores for tertiary institutions across the country. These scores serve as the regulatory floor for all universities, polytechnics, and monotechnics.

As of the announcement, the approved thresholds are: Universities - 150; Colleges of Nursing - 140; and Polytechnics - 100. These figures were decided after a comprehensive review of the national average performance in the 2026 UTME, aimed at ensuring that institutions maintain a balance between accessibility and academic standards.
