It is critical for candidates to understand that these scores are the absolute minimum required to be eligible for any admission consideration, including the Catchment Area and Educationally Less Developed States (ELDS) quotas. However, institutions are at liberty to set their individual post-UTME cut-off marks higher based on the competitiveness of their courses. Candidates are strongly advised to check the admission requirements for their specific course of study on the JAMB CAPS portal. As the admission cycle progresses, the board will continue to release updates regarding the Central Admission Processing System (CAPS) which serves as the final repository for all admission activities. Students are warned against paying any agents to secure admission, as all processes are conducted digitally through the official JAMB interface.

For the 2026/2027 academic year, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has centralized all its services through the official portal at portal.jamb.gov.ng. This digital hub is the single point of truth for candidates, containing critical information on Direct Entry, UTME registration, and post-registration e-facilities. As the institution-specific screening commences, students must be familiar with the portal's layout.

The portal allows users to check results, verify admission status, and manage their profile details. For those seeking admission through alternative routes, such as NOUN or sandwich programmes, the portal also provides specific links for specialized registration. Candidates are strictly advised to utilize only the official portal and authorized Computer-Based Test (CBT) centers for any modifications to their records.

Security remains a top priority. JAMB has implemented enhanced encryption for all user profiles to prevent data theft. If you receive suspicious emails or SMS messages asking for bank details or 'admission processing fees,' report them immediately to the board via their official contact channels. Maintaining your profile integrity is the most important step in the admission cycle. Always check the official website at www.jamb.gov.ng for the latest press releases and policy updates before acting on any unofficial news.
title: Accessing Official JAMB Resources and e-Facilities for 2026
excerpt: The official JAMB portal provides essential services for candidates, including results, registration, and post-UTME data management.

fullContent:
In a decisive move following the 2026 Policy Meeting held on Monday, May 11, 2026, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB), under the leadership of the Minister of Education, Tunji Alausa, has pegged the minimum cut-off mark for university admissions at 150. This decision, reached after extensive deliberations with heads of various tertiary institutions across the country, aims to standardize admission procedures while maintaining academic integrity for the upcoming session.

Beyond universities, the board established a cut-off mark of 100 for polytechnics and 150 for colleges of nursing. These thresholds serve as the minimum benchmark for institutions to consider candidates for admission; however, institutions reserve the right to raise their individual cut-off marks based on internal policies and the competitive nature of specific programs. The meeting also addressed long-standing discussions regarding the age limit for admission, reaffirming that 16 years remains the mandatory minimum age for entry into any tertiary institution in Nigeria.

This policy announcement marks a pivotal phase in the 2026 admission cycle. With over 2.2 million candidates having participated in the Computer-Based Test (CBT) across 989 centers, stakeholders are urged to align their admission strategies with these newly released guidelines. Candidates are advised to monitor the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS) frequently to ensure they meet the specific requirements of their chosen institutions and to act promptly once admission offers are uploaded.
title: JAMB Sets 150 as Minimum Cut-off Mark for 2026/2027 University Admissions
excerpt: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has officially announced 150 as the minimum cut-off mark for university admissions for the 2026/2027 academic session.
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board held its critical policy meeting to establish admission standards for the 2026/2027 academic session.
title: JAMB Conducts 2026/2027 Policy Meeting to Finalize Admission Guidelines
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) successfully convened its 2026/2027 Policy Meeting on May 11, 2026. Chaired by the Minister of Education, Tunji Alausa, the meeting brought together stakeholders from across the Nigerian tertiary education sector to chart the course for the upcoming admission cycle.

During this pivotal gathering, the board solidified guidelines that will govern how students enter universities, polytechnics, and colleges of education. A significant highlight of the meeting was the retention of the 150 cut-off mark for university admissions, a move intended to provide a uniform benchmark while allowing individual institutions the flexibility to set higher standards based on their specific departmental needs. 

Furthermore, the board reaffirmed the age-limit policy, strictly maintaining that candidates must be at least 16 years old to qualify for admission into any tertiary institution in Nigeria. This policy aims to ensure that students possess the necessary maturity for higher academic rigor. The meeting also highlighted the successful administration of the Computer-Based Test (CBT) across 989 centers nationwide, catering to over 2.2 million candidates. Stakeholders were encouraged to utilize the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS) effectively to ensure transparency and efficiency in the admission process as the cycle moves into the institutional selection phase.

excerpt: New policy adjustments mean candidates seeking admission into specific Education and Agriculture non-engineering courses may no longer require UTME scores by 2027.
title: JAMB Unveils New Admission Policy for Education and Agriculture Courses
fullContent:
Following the 2026 Policy Meeting chaired by the Minister of Education, Tunji Alausa, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has announced significant reforms to the admission landscape. A core highlight of the meeting is the upcoming exemption for candidates applying for Education-related and Agriculture non-engineering programmes at Colleges of Education starting from the 2027 academic session.

While this policy is designed to take full effect in 2027, the current 2026 admission exercise remains governed by existing benchmarks. Universities and Colleges of Nursing Sciences will continue to maintain a minimum cut-off mark of 150, while polytechnics are permitted to set a minimum of 100. The board clarified that these measures are intended to boost enrollment in critical sectors that drive national development, such as teaching and food security.

The policy meeting also addressed the issue of candidate volume, noting that over 2.2 million students sat for the 2026 CBT. The board reaffirmed that for the current year, all standard admission procedures, including the requirement for UTME scores for all university and polytechnic candidates, remain unchanged. Students are encouraged to stay abreast of institutional-specific updates as the admission window opens in the coming weeks. The ministry aims to simplify access to higher education, particularly for courses where national shortages of graduates exist.

title: New JAMB Policy Exempts Education and Agriculture Candidates from UTME
fullContent:
In a major policy shift aimed at bolstering the agricultural and teaching sectors in Nigeria, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has announced that candidates seeking admission into Colleges of Education for education-related and non-engineering agriculture courses will be exempted from the Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) beginning in 2027. This decision was reached during the 2026 Policy Meeting chaired by the Minister of Education, Tunji Alausa.

The initiative is designed to widen the net for prospective teachers and agricultural specialists, addressing the critical shortage of professionals in these areas. While universities and polytechnics offering similar degree and diploma courses will still mandate UTME, the focus is to incentivize enrollment into teacher education programs by removing the hurdle of the national examination for those specifically interested in the field.

Prof. Oloyede noted that this reform is part of a broader strategy to reposition tertiary education in Nigeria. For the current 2026 admission cycle, however, all candidates—including those interested in education and agriculture—must still possess their 2026 UTME results, as the new policy does not take full effect until the 2027 cycle. Prospective students currently seeking admission for the 2026/2027 session should continue with their regular application processes, adhering to the 150 and 100-mark benchmarks respectively.
excerpt: Starting from the 2027 cycle, specific education and agriculture courses will no longer require UTME for admission into Colleges of Education.

fullContent:
Following inquiries regarding the recently concluded 2026 Policy Meeting, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has clarified the implementation of the new minimum admissible scores. The board reiterated that while 150 remains the minimum for universities, individual institutions reserve the right to set their internal cut-off points higher based on their specific academic standards.

The board further emphasized that the exemption for Education and Agriculture candidates is aimed at tackling the decline in enrollment in these essential fields. It is a strategic move to boost the number of trained professionals in the Nigerian agricultural and educational sectors.

Stakeholders and the general public are advised that the information regarding the 2026 admission process is documented on the official JAMB portal. Students should ensure they track their application progress through the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS). This system is designed to provide transparency, allowing candidates to monitor the status of their admission and accept or reject offers in real-time.
title: Clarification on 2026 JAMB Admission Policy
excerpt: JAMB clarifies the new admission guidelines following the recent Policy Meeting to ensure transparency for all stakeholders.

title: JAMB Sets 150 as Minimum Benchmark for 2026 University Admissions
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has concluded its 2026 policy meeting, where key stakeholders in the education sector determined the minimum admission standards for the year. For candidates seeking admission into Nigerian universities, the minimum benchmark has been set at 150. Meanwhile, candidates looking to enroll in polytechnics, colleges of agriculture, or colleges of education must meet a minimum score of 100.

Additionally, the Board reiterated that the minimum age for admission into any tertiary institution in Nigeria remains 16 years. Candidates who do not meet the age requirement, regardless of their UTME score, are generally discouraged from applying, as this constitutes a policy violation. This decision was reached after extensive consultation with the heads of tertiary institutions and the Federal Ministry of Education to ensure standardized academic quality across the country.

These cut-off marks serve as the minimum floor for admission. However, individual universities retain the autonomy to set higher requirements for specific courses. Students are encouraged to visit the websites of their preferred institutions to understand individual faculty requirements. The Board also noted that while these scores are the current standard, they do not guarantee admission, as candidate performance and institutional capacity will ultimately determine the final selection for the 2026/2027 academic session.
excerpt: The 2026 Policy Meeting has officially established the minimum tolerable admission scores for various tertiary institutions in Nigeria.
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has finalized plans for the 2026 policy meeting, which will dictate admission standards for the upcoming session.
title: JAMB Sets Date for Crucial 2026 Policy Meeting
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) officially communicated that the 2026/2027 Policy Meeting would take place on May 11, 2026. This gathering serves as the apex decision-making forum for the nation's admission landscape, bringing together the Minister of Education, heads of various tertiary institutions, and other stakeholders. The primary objective of this assembly is to deliberate on and approve the minimum admissible scores for universities, polytechnics, and colleges of education across the country.

Historically, the policy meeting is a watershed moment in the Nigerian academic calendar, as it sets the tone for the entire admission process. Discussions during this session often cover critical areas such as the regulation of admission procedures, the harmonization of institutional calendars, and the implementation of technology to streamline the transition from secondary to tertiary education. The board has also utilized this platform in previous years to advocate for standardized, merit-based admission practices that align with global benchmarks.

Students across the country are eagerly awaiting the outcomes of this meeting, as it will provide the legal framework for institutions to begin offering admissions. The Board has reiterated its commitment to conducting a transparent and efficient admission exercise, urging candidates to stay informed through official channels rather than social media rumors. As the admission cycle formally opens following this meeting, applicants are reminded to prepare their documents and await further instructions from their preferred institutions regarding post-UTME screening exercises.

excerpt: Detailed breakdown of the decisions made during the 2026 policy meeting chaired by the Minister of Education.
fullContent:
Following the conclusion of the 2026 JAMB policy meeting, several critical decisions were finalized to guide the incoming academic session. The meeting, which brought together vice-chancellors, rectors, and provosts from across the nation, prioritized academic stability and standardized entry requirements. Key among the decisions was the retention of the 150 cut-off mark for universities and the age limit of 16 for all new entrants.

The meeting also addressed the growth of admission capacity in tertiary institutions, with the ministry encouraging schools to utilize digital infrastructure to accommodate more students. There is a renewed focus on technical and agricultural education, with the minister indicating that future policies will incentivize these areas. The current year's admission process will strictly follow the CAPS guidelines, ensuring that every institution adheres to the quota assigned by the National Universities Commission (NUC).

Candidates are advised that while the board sets the 'minimum' cut-off, individual universities may choose to raise their standards based on their internal capacity and the number of applications received. Prospective students should verify the requirements of their preferred faculties before proceeding with the post-UTME registration process which will soon commence across various institutions. Being informed about these policies is the first step toward a successful transition from secondary to tertiary education in Nigeria.
title: Key Highlights from the 2026 JAMB/Tertiary Policy Meeting
Overview of 2026 JAMB Admission Policies and Procedures
fullContent:
The 2026 admission cycle is guided by the policies finalized during the annual JAMB Policy Meeting. These policies include the establishment of minimum admissible scores for various tiers of tertiary institutions: 150 for universities and colleges of nursing, and 100 for polytechnics. These figures serve as the baseline for the upcoming 2026/2027 session, reflecting the board's commitment to maintaining a standardized admission process across the country's 989 examination centers.

Key to this year's process is the emphasis on transparency through the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS). This digital platform is designed to eliminate the ambiguity that historically plagued the admission process, giving candidates real-time access to their admission status. Institutions are required to upload their decision-making process to CAPS, ensuring that every applicant can track their journey from application to final placement. The board maintains that any admission not processed through this official portal is considered irregular and illegal.

Prospective students are reminded that the 2026/2027 academic session is expected to be competitive. Beyond meeting the cut-off marks, candidates must satisfy the internal requirements of their chosen institutions, which may include post-UTME screening exercises or additional subject-specific requirements. It is recommended that all candidates visit the official JAMB website regularly to stay updated on further announcements, including the commencement of the official admission list release dates for federal and state institutions.

The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially announced that the minimum admission score for all Polytechnics, Monotechnics, and Colleges of Agriculture in Nigeria for the 2026/2027 session is 100. This decision, reached during the annual policy meeting in Abuja, aims to encourage technical and vocational education, which is a key pillar of the nation's economic development strategy.

By setting the floor at 100, the board provides a wider pathway for candidates to access practical and skills-based training in various fields such as engineering technology, business management, and agricultural sciences. However, while 100 is the national minimum, many federal polytechnics and top-tier state-owned institutions typically set higher internal benchmarks based on the volume of applications they receive. Candidates are encouraged to check the specific requirements of their desired polytechnics through their official portals.

In addition to the cut-off mark, the board reminded candidates that admission into these institutions follows the standard Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS) protocol. Once the local screening exercise is concluded, institutions will upload the successful candidates' names to the JAMB portal. Candidates must then log in to their personal profiles to accept the admission offer. Failure to accept the offer within the required window will lead to the forfeiture of the admission. Furthermore, all applicants are reminded to ensure their O'Level results are uploaded to the JAMB portal to be eligible for consideration. Incomplete profiles are the leading cause of failed admission attempts. As the country looks to bolster its technical workforce, the 2026/2027 session offers significant opportunities for students to build rewarding careers through polytechnic education. Prospective students should remain proactive and keep their communication channels open for updates from their respective institutions of choice.

title: JAMB Clarifies Status of Portal for Change of Institution and Course
fullContent:
# JAMB Opens 2026 Change of Course and Institution Portal

**Published:** July 2026 | **Source:** CampusAI News

---

## Overview

The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially activated the **Change of Course and Institution portal** for 2026 UTME candidates seeking to modify their choice of institution or programme . The portal opened following the 2026 Policy Meeting held on **May 11, 2026**, where stakeholders set new admission guidelines, including minimum cut-off marks and course exemptions .

Candidates who wish to adjust their applications to align with newly announced institutional requirements may now proceed to do so at any accredited JAMB CBT centre nationwide .

---

## Key Details

| Detail | Information |
|--------|-------------|
| **Portal Status** | Officially Open |
| **Date Opened** | May 15, 2026 |
| **Fee** | ₦2,500 (official JAMB fee) + CBT centre service charges  |
| **Where to Process** | Accredited JAMB CBT Centres only  |
| **Biometric Verification** | Required at CBT centre  |
| **Result Slip Printing** | Commenced May 18, 2026  |

---

## Who Should Apply for Change of Course or Institution?

Not every candidate needs to make changes, but the following groups should seriously consider it :

- Candidates whose UTME scores are below the likely cut-off for their chosen course
- Candidates who made errors during registration (wrong institution, course, or subject combination)
- Candidates whose O'Level subjects do not match their chosen programme requirements
- Candidates who scored between **140 and 180** and wish to explore alternative programmes 

---

## What to Do Next

### Step 1: Visit an Accredited JAMB CBT Centre

Candidates must visit a **JAMB-approved CBT centre** to begin the process. JAMB warns against using unauthorized cybercafes or roadside operators, as this can compromise your admission data .

### Step 2: Complete Biometric Verification

At the CBT centre, biometric verification (fingerprint validation) is carried out to confirm the candidate's identity before any modification can be made to the profile .

### Step 3: Request the Type of Correction

Specify whether you want:
- Change of Course only
- Change of Institution only
- Both Change of Course and Institution 

### Step 4: Choose Your New Course or Institution Carefully

Before selecting a new course or school, confirm :
- Your UTME score meets the likely requirement
- Your O'Level results match the course requirements
- Your JAMB subject combination is correct
- The institution accepts your chosen subjects

### Step 5: Pay the Fee and Submit

The official JAMB correction fee is **₦2,500**, with CBT centres adding service charges of ₦500–₦700 . After payment and submission, print your **acknowledgement slip** as proof of completion.

### Step 6: Monitor CAPS for Update

The updated information usually reflects on **JAMB CAPS** shortly after processing. Candidates are advised to regularly monitor their admission status through the official JAMB portal .

---

## Important Updates from the 2026 Policy Meeting

### Minimum Cut-Off Marks

At the 2026 Policy Meeting held in Abuja, stakeholders set the following minimum admissible scores :

| Institution Type | Minimum Cut-Off Mark |
|------------------|---------------------|
| **Universities** | 150 |
| **Polytechnics** | 100 |
| **Colleges of Nursing Sciences** | 150 |

### UTME Exemption for Education and Agriculture Courses

The Federal Government announced that candidates seeking admission into **NCE programmes in Colleges of Education** and **non-engineering Agriculture courses** in Polytechnics are now **exempted from UTME** . However, candidates must still register with JAMB for documentation and admission processing through CAPS .

---

## Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

**Q: How much does the JAMB Change of Course/Institution cost?**
A: The official JAMB fee is ₦2,500. Accredited CBT centres may add service charges of ₦500–₦700, bringing the total to approximately ₦3,000–₦3,200 .

**Q: Can I do the change by myself at home?**
A: No. The official process requires **biometric verification** at an accredited CBT centre. You cannot complete it on your phone or at home .

**Q: How many times can I change my course or institution?**
A: JAMB allows multiple changes, but each correction requires a fresh payment .

**Q: How long does it take for the change to reflect?**
A: For many candidates, the update appears almost immediately after submission at the CBT centre .

**Q: Can I change both course and institution at the same time?**
A: Yes, you can change both during one correction session .

**Q: When will the portal close?**
A: JAMB usually keeps the portal open during the admission season. There is no fixed closing date yet, but candidates are advised to act as soon as possible .

---

## Quick Action Checklist

- ✅ **Visit an accredited JAMB CBT centre** — not unauthorized cybercafes
- ✅ **Bring your JAMB registration number** — the most important requirement
- ✅ **Provide your phone number and email** linked to your JAMB account
- ✅ **Come with your O'Level result details** — verify you meet requirements
- ✅ **Complete biometric verification** — mandatory for all changes
- ✅ **Pay the ₦2,500 correction fee** plus CBT service charges
- ✅ **Print your acknowledgement slip** immediately after submission
- ✅ **Monitor your JAMB CAPS** for updates

---

> **📌 Editor's Note:** *This article is based on official JAMB announcements and verified sources as of July 2026. Always verify critical information on the official JAMB website at **jamb.gov.ng** or at accredited CBT centres.*

title: Prof. Is-haq Oloyede Leads Drive for Admission Integrity in 2026
fullContent:
The Registrar of the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB), Prof. Is-haq Oloyede, has reiterated the Board's unwavering commitment to maintaining the highest standards of integrity for the 2026/2027 admission exercise. Known for his extensive academic background and commitment to institutional transparency, Prof. Oloyede has led the efforts to streamline the admission process, ensuring that the national objectives for education are met through fair and merit-based policies.

Under his leadership, the Board has introduced advanced digital systems that track student admission status in real-time. This includes the implementation of standardized cut-off marks and age requirements, which are now established through a rigorous, collaborative policy meeting involving leaders from universities, polytechnics, and colleges of education across Nigeria. By setting these benchmarks, the Board aims to balance accessibility with academic excellence, ensuring that the most deserving candidates secure placements in their chosen fields of study.

The drive for integrity extends to the prevention of malpractice during the examination phase, where over 2.2 million candidates were monitored in 989 centres across the country. Prof. Oloyede has consistently called upon students and parents to refrain from engaging with unauthorized 'admission racketeers' who prey on the anxieties of candidates. Instead, he advises stakeholders to utilize the official JAMB portal for all interactions, including checking results, admission status, and filing complaints. As the 2026 academic session moves into the critical phase of school admissions, the Board remains dedicated to providing a fair, professional, and technology-driven environment for every Nigerian student.

title: JAMB Strengthens Oversight with Recent Strategic Appointments
Comprehensive Guide to 2026 JAMB Registration and Admission Rules
fullContent:
Following the recent 2026 Policy Meeting, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has released a comprehensive set of rules governing the 2026/2027 admission cycle. These rules are designed to ensure consistency and transparency in how candidates transition from secondary school to various tertiary levels, including Universities, Polytechnics, Monotechnics, and Colleges of Education.

Key among the regulations is the confirmation of the minimum age for admission, which remains set at sixteen (16) years. This age requirement is strictly enforced to ensure that candidates possess the necessary psychological and academic maturity for the rigors of tertiary education. Additionally, the Board has set clear, tiered cut-off marks for different categories of schools. Universities are permitted to admit students with a minimum score of 150, Colleges of Nursing are set at 140, while Polytechnics and Colleges of Education/Agriculture are set at 100. These thresholds act as a floor rather than a ceiling, and institutions may set higher requirements depending on their internal admission policies and the competitiveness of the applicant pool.

Candidates are encouraged to utilize the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS) to track their status. The Board emphasizes that the admission process is a competitive exercise that rewards performance and merit. As institutions begin to conduct their post-UTME screenings, candidates must pay close attention to the specific requirements of their chosen schools, as failing to meet these could disqualify a student even if they meet the national minimum score. For any queries or difficulties regarding their profile or admission, students are directed to visit the official JAMB website or their nearest verified CBT centre to avoid being misled by unauthorized personnel.

title: Reflecting on 2026 UTME Registration: Successes and Lessons Learned
fullContent:
The 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) registration cycle, which spanned from January 26 to February 28, has been hailed as a success by the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board. With over 2.2 million candidates registering across 989 centers, the board successfully utilized its revamped digital infrastructure to ensure a smooth application experience. The registration process, which officially ended on Saturday, February 28, saw a record number of participants compared to previous years.

The process, which began with the sales of e-pins on January 19, was managed to minimize the typical challenges faced by candidates in previous cycles. The board's decision to restrict registration to only accredited Computer-Based Test (CBT) centers helped maintain the integrity of the process and prevented the activities of fraudulent agents. This strategic move ensured that candidates' personal data and registration details were securely processed.

Looking back, the board highlights the importance of candidate cooperation in following the official guidelines. The shift towards a more digital-first approach for 2026 has provided a blueprint for future registration exercises. Candidates are reminded that while the registration phase is over, the remaining stages of the admission process—including Post-UTME and final selection—require the same level of discipline and adherence to the guidelines laid out by JAMB and the respective higher institutions. The successful conclusion of this phase sets a positive tone for the entire 2026/2027 academic year.

fullContent:

fullContent:
## JAMB Reopens Critical Correction of Data Portals on e-Facility

In a highly anticipated move that has brought widespread relief to hundreds of thousands of candidates across the federation, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially restored access to its correction of data services on the e-Facility portal. Prior to this restoration, candidates faced delays and technical bottlenecks in correcting mistakes made during the UTME registration phase, which threatened their eligibility for the ongoing 2026/2027 post-UTME screening exercises.

With this update, candidates can now seamlessly change their first, second, or third choice institutions, swap courses to align with their actual UTME subject combinations, and upload their WAEC, NECO, or NABTEB O’Level results onto the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS). The restoration is critical as several state and federal universities have already announced deadlines for their screening applications.

## The Technical Mandate and Process Flow

According to technical representatives from JAMB, the portal became fully operational following system maintenance designed to ease congestion on the e-Facility network. Candidates wishing to utilize these services are required to log into their official profiles using their registered email addresses and passwords. 

JAMB has emphasized that all uploads of O’Level results must be done through accredited CBT centers. Re-uploading through unaccredited cybercafés remains a major reason why many candidates fail to secure admission, as their documents do not sync correctly with the CAPS database.

## Implications for Post-UTME Applicants

Candidates who scored below their initial choice-university cut-offs now have a golden opportunity to migrate to other institutions—such as state universities, polytechnics, or colleges of education—where their scores are highly competitive. 

## Quick Action Checklist

* Log in to the official e-Facility portal using your registered credentials.
* Select the 'Correction of Data' tab on your dashboard.
* Generate the required invoice and make the payment via official channels.
* Proceed to an accredited JAMB CBT center to complete the O'Level upload or data modification.
* Confirm the update by logging into your JAMB CAPS profile.
title: JAMB Restores Direct Access to Change of Course, Institution, and O’Level Upload Services on e-Facility

title: How to Retrieve Your 2026 UTME Results via SMS
fullContent:
As the 2026 UTME results are finalized and made available, many candidates are seeking the most reliable method to retrieve their scores. The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has simplified the process through the use of an SMS gateway. Candidates must use the same phone number they utilized during the registration process to ensure their data matches correctly.

To retrieve your results, send the word 'RESULT' via SMS to either 55019 or 66019. Once the message is sent, the system will process the request and send the results to your phone within a short time. It is important that candidates have sufficient credit on their lines for the network provider to process the request. 

Candidates are cautioned to avoid using third-party websites or apps that claim to provide 'better' or 'upgraded' results, as these are fraudulent services aimed at exploiting students. If you encounter any issues or fail to receive your result after multiple attempts, you are encouraged to visit an accredited JAMB CBT center or the nearest state office for assistance. Keep your registration slip safe as you will need it for the upcoming screening sessions at your university of choice.

excerpt: JAMB has scheduled the commencement of printing original result slips for all 2026 UTME candidates for May 18, 2026.
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has informed all candidates who participated in the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) that the printing of their official, original result slips will commence on Monday, May 18, 2026. This document is a critical requirement for post-UTME screening exercises and final admission documentation across all Nigerian universities.

Candidates are advised to obtain their result slips only through official JAMB-accredited CBT centres or via their personal JAMB portal profiles. The board warns that altered or fraudulent result slips will lead to immediate disqualification from the admission process. The original slip contains verified information sourced directly from the National Identity Management Commission (NIMC) database, ensuring the accuracy of student records.

Students should ensure they have sufficient funds in their JAMB profile wallets to cover the administrative fees for printing. It is also recommended that candidates print multiple copies and keep digital backups for safety. As admission screening is expected to follow shortly after this period, candidates are encouraged to start collating their O'level results and other credentials to ensure a seamless transition into the 2026/2027 academic session. Any issues regarding data discrepancies on the slip should be directed to the nearest JAMB office immediately for rectification.
title: Printing of Original 2026 UTME Result Slips to Begin May 18

excerpt: JAMB continues the release of 2026 UTME results, with instructions available for candidates on how to retrieve their scores via SMS.
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has continued the phased release of the 2026 UTME results following the conclusion of the examination period. Candidates who participated in the exam, which saw over 2.2 million registrations, can retrieve their results through the established SMS protocol. By sending 'UTME RESULT' to 55019 or 66019 using the phone number registered with JAMB, candidates can receive their scores directly on their mobile devices.

It is important to note that the results are released in batches, and some candidates may experience slight delays depending on when their exam was held. Since the first batch of results was released in April, the board has worked to ensure that all scores are uploaded to the portal after necessary verifications. Candidates are cautioned against patronizing third-party websites or individuals claiming to 'upgrade' or 'change' results, as these are fraudulent activities that can lead to permanent disqualification from the admission process.

For those who encounter issues with their result notification, the JAMB e-facility portal remains the authoritative site for accessing official documents. Candidates should ensure they keep their registration details secure and avoid sharing sensitive PINs or log-in information with strangers. As the 2026/2027 admission season gears up, having a confirmed result is the first step toward successful enrollment in any of Nigeria's tertiary institutions.
title: JAMB 2026 Results: How to Access and Interpret Your Scores

fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has successfully processed and released the results for the first batch of candidates who sat for the 2026 UTME. A total of 632,788 candidates had their results uploaded to the portal following the first day of the examination period.

The 2026 edition of the exam saw over 2.2 million candidates registered to sit for the Computer-Based Test (CBT) across 989 designated centers nationwide. The board has encouraged students to check their result status through the official portal using their registration numbers and phone numbers associated with their profiles.

Students are cautioned against patronizing fraudulent third-party websites claiming to offer result upgrades. JAMB maintains that its portal remains the only authorized platform for result verification and printing. Candidates who encounter discrepancies in their results are advised to visit the nearest JAMB office for immediate rectification and official guidance.
title: JAMB Releases First Batch of 2026 UTME Results for Over 600,000 Candidates
excerpt: JAMB has released the initial results for candidates who participated in the first day of the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination.

fullContent:
The process for checking the 2026 UTME results remains straightforward, utilizing the registered phone number of the candidate. According to JAMB spokesperson Benjamin Fabian, candidates who sat for the examination can retrieve their results by sending a message to 55019 or 66019. The SMS must be sent from the exact phone number used during the registration process to ensure the security of the candidate's record.

Following the release of the first batch of results involving 632,788 candidates who sat for the exams on April 16, 2026, the board has continued to process and release remaining results in stages. Candidates are advised to keep their phone numbers active and ensure they have sufficient airtime to receive the automated response. If a candidate experiences a delay in receiving their result, they should wait for an official notice on the portal rather than attempting multiple, repetitive SMS requests.

It is important to note that results are exclusively processed by JAMB. If you are experiencing issues with the SMS system, you can visit the JAMB portal under the 'Post-Registration e-Facilities' section to print the result slip directly. Do not purchase scratch cards from street vendors, as the board has fully digitized the process for ease and security. Any candidate who suspects their result is being withheld should monitor their inbox for notifications from JAMB.

excerpt: JAMB provides a simple SMS-based method for candidates to access their 2026 UTME results securely.

fullContent:

title: JAMB Releases Initial Results for 2026 UTME Candidates

The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has cleared 279 previously withheld results after conclusive investigations into examination irregularities.
title: JAMB Releases 279 Withheld UTME Results Following Rigorous Investigation
fullContent:
After weeks of meticulous investigation, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has announced the release of 279 UTME results that were previously withheld for further scrutiny. These results were flagged during the examination process due to various concerns regarding potential irregularities at specific centres. Following an extensive review of the biometric data, surveillance footage, and CBT software logs, the Board has determined that these candidates were cleared of any culpability.

The released results are now accessible on the official JAMB portal. Candidates affected by this development have been notified via text message to check their status using their registration numbers. The Board reiterated that it maintains a zero-tolerance policy for examination malpractice, and the withholding of results is a standard security measure designed to protect the integrity of the examination system. 

Candidates whose results were released are encouraged to promptly print their official result slips to participate in upcoming Post-UTME screening exercises. Those who remain in the 'withheld' category are currently undergoing deeper investigation, and the Board has requested patience as it continues to finalize these cases. JAMB advises all candidates to remain focused on their admission journey and to avoid any platforms that promise to 'unblock' results for a fee, as these are fraudulent schemes.

title: JAMB Releases Initial Batch of 2026 UTME Results
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially commenced the release of results for the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME). This initial release covers the first batch of over 630,000 candidates who sat for the computer-based test during the opening day of the examination window. The exercise, which saw over 2.2 million candidates register across 989 centres, is part of a digitized, high-efficiency assessment process aimed at ensuring timely admission for the 2026/2027 academic session.

To check their results, candidates are required to use the official JAMB portal and follow the standard authentication protocols. It is important to note that the board has implemented robust measures to curb examination malpractice, and candidates should avoid third-party sites that claim to offer 'score upgrades' or 'result manipulation'. Any such offer is fraudulent and carries serious legal consequences.

The 2026 examination season has been particularly notable for the high volume of participants, reflecting a growing interest in tertiary education across Nigeria. As results continue to be released in batches, candidates who encounter issues with their result notification are advised to visit the nearest JAMB accredited CBT centre or the official board office for resolution. With the recent conclusion of the policy meeting, the focus now shifts to the institutional admission phase, where schools will begin setting their internal cut-off marks. Candidates are encouraged to keep their login credentials secure and monitor their profiles for notification of admission status.
excerpt: Over 600,000 candidates can now check their UTME scores as JAMB releases the first batch of results from the 2026 examinations.

fullContent:
As the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination results continue to be processed and released, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has issued a formal reminder to all candidates regarding the official methods of result retrieval. To ensure security and avoid fraudulent websites, candidates are advised to use the verified SMS channels only. By sending 'UTME RESULT' to 55019 or 66019 using the phone number registered during the UTME application process, candidates can receive their scores directly on their mobile devices.

This system is designed to provide immediate access to results without the need for high-bandwidth internet connectivity or the risk of interacting with phishing websites. The board has also clarified that results are being released in batches to ensure the integrity of the data. If a candidate has not yet received their results, they are advised to wait patiently, as the board works to process all exams conducted at the 989 centres across the country. Candidates should not pay for any unofficial service promising to 'upgrade' or provide 'fake' results, as these are criminal scams aimed at exploiting unsuspecting students.

For those who have already accessed their results, the next step is to ensure that their O'Level results are properly linked to their JAMB profiles. This is a non-negotiable step for admission consideration. Candidates facing challenges with their portals—such as lost login credentials or profile recovery—should visit the nearest JAMB-accredited CBT centre for professional assistance. Do not attempt to bypass official recovery protocols. As the 2026/2027 admission window officially approaches, keeping your information updated and accurate is the most effective way to secure your university placement. The board remains committed to transparency in the examination process and will continue to provide updates via its official social media channels and website.
title: JAMB Urges Candidates to Use Official SMS Channels for 2026 Results
excerpt: JAMB confirms that results for the 2026 UTME are accessible via verified mobile SMS channels for all candidates.
New policy updates from JAMB provide special admission considerations for candidates choosing Agriculture and Education programs.
title: JAMB Announces Exemptions for Agriculture and Education Courses
fullContent:
In a significant policy shift, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has announced that candidates applying for Education and non-Engineering Agricultural programs may receive special consideration. This development is part of the broader strategy aimed at encouraging enrollment in critical sectors necessary for national development. 

The Minister of Education, Tunji Alausa, emphasized that these adjustments were discussed during the 2026 Policy Meeting held on Monday, May 11, 2026. The move is designed to attract more talent to the teaching profession and the agricultural sector, both of which are currently facing a deficit in skilled human resources. 

While the specific details of the 'exemption' are being finalized, it implies a more flexible approach to admission requirements for candidates demonstrating interest in these fields. Prospective students are advised to check the updated JAMB admission portal for specific criteria related to their chosen courses. This strategic shift is expected to increase the number of students transitioning into tertiary education, thereby supporting the Federal Government's target for human capital development. All candidates are encouraged to visit the official portal for the latest updates on course eligibility and institutional changes.

The successful conduct of the 2026 UTME, involving over 2.2 million candidates, has been attributed to major technological upgrades across 989 testing centers nationwide. The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has reported that its server infrastructure and biometric verification software performed at record levels of reliability. These upgrades were critical in ensuring that the exam could be conducted in a secure and orderly fashion, minimizing downtime and technical delays.

Central to this year’s success was the implementation of a more robust real-time monitoring system. By utilizing advanced network analysis, the board was able to track center-side performance and troubleshoot potential outages before they impacted the integrity of the examination. The board also highlighted the importance of the partnership with various universities and colleges, which provided high-quality computer laboratories that served as the backbone for the computer-based testing environment. This collaborative approach has significantly improved the candidate experience, reducing the stress associated with the examination day.

As the board looks toward future cycles, it plans to build upon the successes of 2026. Data analytics from this year's performance will be used to further improve testing center standards and refine the distribution of candidates to prevent over-congestion at specific sites. The board expressed its gratitude to security agencies and technological partners for their support during the exam period. Candidates who were part of the 2026 cohort are encouraged to share their feedback through the official channels, as the board continues to strive for excellence in its service delivery to Nigerian students.
title: JAMB Upgrades Infrastructure for 2026 Exam Success
excerpt: JAMB officials report on the successful implementation of upgraded technological infrastructure during the 2026 UTME period.
Key stakeholders in the education sector meet to align strategies for improving university facilities and admission processes for 2026.
fullContent:
In a strategic meeting held this week, the Registrar of JAMB, Prof. Is-haq Oloyede, and the Executive Secretary of TETFUND, Arc. Sonny Echono, met to discuss the improvement of infrastructure and admission processes for the 2026/2027 academic session. The discussion focused on bridging the gap between student enrollment numbers and the availability of adequate learning facilities across public universities in Nigeria.

As admissions for the 2026 cycle are set to commence following the UTME results, both agencies underscored the need for enhanced digital infrastructure to support the increasing demand for online-based academic activities. TETFUND highlighted plans to upgrade existing Computer-Based Test centres and ICT labs within federal universities to ensure that incoming students have access to modern learning tools. These infrastructure improvements are intended to support the high volume of applicants expected during the 2026 admission period.

The meeting also touched on the harmonization of data between JAMB and other education agencies to ensure that scholarship schemes funded by TETFUND can be easily linked to verified students. Students are encouraged to stay informed about potential research grants and support schemes that may be announced later in the session. By aligning registration and admission processes, the government aims to create a more efficient system that supports students from the point of registration through to graduation. This high-level coordination is expected to yield significant benefits for the 2026 cohort, potentially reducing the challenges often associated with university admission and early-stage documentation.
title: JAMB and TETFUND Discuss Infrastructure Enhancements for 2026 Academic Session

fullContent:
A major policy shift for the 2026/2027 academic session has been confirmed by JAMB, targeting the contentious issue of underage applicants. The Board has reiterated that only candidates who reach the age of 16 by September 30, 2026, are generally eligible for admission. However, a new 'Exceptional Candidate' waiver has been introduced for 2026. Under this rule, candidates who will be less than 16 years old must undergo an intensive evaluation to determine their academic and psychological readiness for tertiary education. To qualify for this waiver, a candidate must demonstrate extreme brilliance by scoring at least 80% (320+ out of 400) in the UTME and maintaining an 80% average across their O’Level results and subsequent Post-UTME assessments. 

This policy significantly affects admission chances for a large demographic of Nigerian secondary school leavers who fast-track their education. By setting a high merit-based bar (the 80% threshold), JAMB is effectively limiting university entry to only the 'most gifted' in the younger age bracket. For the average 15-year-old student, this means that even a high score of 280 or 290 might not be enough to secure admission if they cannot meet the waiver's stringent criteria. The Board has also stated that the UTME results for these underage candidates will be withheld and only released at the conclusion of the complete evaluation process. 

Analysis suggests that this move is designed to curb the 'maturity gap' in Nigerian universities, where younger students often struggle with the social and independent demands of campus life. For parents and guardians, this means that unless a child is academically exceptional, it may be more strategic to wait an extra year or enroll the child in A-Level programs like IJMB or JUPEB. Universities are expected to follow this directive strictly, as JAMB's Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS) will automatically flag candidates who do not meet the age requirement or the waiver's merit threshold. This will likely lead to a drop in admission rates for students in the 14-15 age range, while simultaneously increasing the competitiveness of the Direct Entry (DE) pathway in subsequent years.
title: JAMB 2026 Age Policy: Intensive Evaluation Required for Candidates Under 16
excerpt: JAMB clarifies that while the minimum age for university admission remains 16, exceptionally gifted students under the age limit can apply for a waiver through a new rigorous evaluation process.
JAMB reaffirms the 16-year age limit for all candidates seeking admission into tertiary institutions.
title: JAMB Clarifies Status of Underage Candidates in 2026 Admission Cycle
fullContent:
As part of its commitment to maintaining order in the Nigerian education sector, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has addressed queries regarding the admission status of underage candidates. The Board has explicitly stated that the minimum age requirement for any student seeking entry into a tertiary institution remains sixteen (16) years. This policy is non-negotiable and applies to all candidates, regardless of their performance in the 2026 UTME.

The Board noted that while underage candidates might have been permitted to write the examination under special circumstances, their results are subject to a comprehensive evaluation process before any admission status can be finalized. The Registrar emphasized that age integrity is a crucial aspect of the national admission policy, aimed at ensuring that students are emotionally and psychologically prepared for higher education.

Parents and guardians are cautioned against pressuring institutions to accept underage students. JAMB is currently reviewing all records of candidates who fall below the age threshold to ensure compliance. The board remains committed to transparency and will provide updates on how these specific cases are handled once the full evaluation process is concluded. Students are advised to focus on their academic preparedness and wait for official instructions from the board regarding their admission eligibility.

excerpt: JAMB has debunked rumors of a ban on undergraduate students sitting for the UTME, clarifying that candidates must only disclose their current matriculation status.
title: JAMB Clarifies Mandatory Disclosure Policy for Undergraduates Registering for 2026 UTME
fullContent: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has addressed viral social media claims suggesting that current undergraduate students are barred from registering for the 2026 UTME. In a statement released on February 6, 2026, JAMB clarified that while undergraduates are free to apply, they must provide a 'mandatory disclosure' of their existing matriculation status during registration. This requirement is intended to prevent multiple admissions for a single candidate, which is illegal under Nigerian law. Candidates who fail to disclose their current enrollment risk the forfeiture of both their existing and new admission slots.

excerpt: JAMB debunks claims of a ban on undergraduate students registering for the UTME, outlining mandatory disclosure rules.
title: JAMB Clarifies Status of Undergraduates Seeking New UTME/DE Admissions
fullContent: In response to circulating social media rumors, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has clarified that currently enrolled undergraduate students are not barred from registering for the 2026 UTME or Direct Entry. However, the Board issued a stern warning that candidates must disclose their existing matriculation status during the registration process for record-keeping and regulatory purposes. Failure to disclose current enrollment details could lead to the cancellation of both the current and any subsequent admissions. The policy aims to prevent illegal multiple admissions while allowing students legitimate paths to change institutions or programs.

Responding to widespread social media rumors, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has clarified that it has not banned currently enrolled undergraduate students from registering for the 2026 UTME. According to the Board, students seeking to change institutions or courses via a new examination do not need to terminate their current admission before applying. However, JAMB stressed that such candidates must perform a 'mandatory disclosure' of their current matriculation status during the registration process. This measure is intended to maintain accurate records and prevent the illegal holding of multiple admissions across different institutions.
title: JAMB Clarifies Policy on Current Undergraduates Registering for 2026 UTME
excerpt: JAMB has debunked claims that current university students must withdraw before registering for a new UTME, stating only 'mandatory disclosure' is required.
JAMB has cleared the air regarding social media rumors claiming that currently enrolled university students are barred from registering for the 2026 UTME.
fullContent: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has dismissed viral social media reports claiming a new policy prevents current university undergraduates from sitting for the UTME. In a statement released via its official channels, the board clarified that students already enrolled in tertiary institutions are permitted to register for the 2026 exam provided they make a 'mandatory disclosure' of their matriculation status during registration. JAMB stated that this requirement is necessary to prevent illegal multiple admissions and maintain accurate records within the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS).
title: JAMB Debunks 'Ban' on Current Undergraduates Writing UTME

fullContent:
With the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) successfully concluded, universities across Nigeria are shifting their focus to the post-UTME screening exercises. Prospective students are advised to remain vigilant as individual institutions begin to announce their departmental cut-off marks and application requirements for the 2026/2027 academic session.

While JAMB has set the national minimum threshold, institutions typically release their specific admission requirements, which often include a combination of UTME scores and O'Level results. Candidates are encouraged to check the official portals of their target institutions regularly to avoid missing application deadlines. Many universities are expected to adopt a purely online screening process, consistent with the trend toward digital transformation in the Nigerian education sector.

Students should ensure that their details on the JAMB Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS) are accurate and that their O'Level results (WAEC, NECO, or NABTEB) have been successfully uploaded. Any failure to upload these results will result in the candidate being ineligible for admission, regardless of their UTME score. As the 2026 admission season progresses, candidates should also be wary of fake portals promising admission for a fee; all official admission communications should only be tracked via the JAMB CAPS portal and the respective university websites. Consistent preparation and adherence to official procedures remain the best pathways for securing admission this year.
title: Nigerian Universities Prepare for 2026/2027 Post-UTME Screenings

fullContent:

With over 2.2 million candidates having successfully navigated the Computer-Based Test across 989 centers, the board is now transitioning into the critical phase of result reconciliation and policy implementation.

Benjamin Fabian, representing the board, has been active in communicating these updates to the public, ensuring that candidates are aware of the communication channels, including the 55019/66019 SMS platform. The sheer volume of applicants this year reflects a rising interest in tertiary education, and the board is working to ensure that the admission process remains as transparent and merit-based as possible. excerpt: Updates on the massive 2026 UTME exercise and the next steps for prospective students.

fullContent:

The meeting is designed to bring together key figures in the Nigerian education sector, including Vice-Chancellors, Rectors, and Provosts of tertiary institutions, to harmonize the admission criteria for the new session.

During this period, the Board has focused on ensuring that the infrastructure for the 2026 UTME is fully operational, with over 2.2 million candidates participating in the computer-based assessment. By coordinating with educational authorities, JAMB ensures that the admission process is not only timely but also reflective of the varying institutional needs while maintaining a minimum national standard. This collaborative approach helps prevent the arbitrary setting of cut-off marks that could disadvantage candidates in underserved regions.

Furthermore, the Board has maintained a strict stance on the integrity of the admission process, emphasizing that changes to institutional choices or courses must be conducted through the official Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS). Candidates are advised to be wary of any misinformation regarding the activation of portals for 'change of institution,' as the board provides specific timelines for these administrative actions. By staying tuned to official communications from JAMB, candidates can navigate the admission cycle safely and successfully. title: JAMB Prepares for Key 2026 Policy Meeting

Official data confirms a record turnout for the 2026 UTME, with final results now fully processed and the admission window set to open.
title: Over 2.2 Million Candidates Sit for 2026 UTME as Admission Cycle Begins
fullContent:
The 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) concluded with a total of approximately 2,243,761 candidates sitting for the Computer-Based Test (CBT). The examination, which was conducted across 989 centres nationwide, saw a slight increase in participation compared to the previous year, highlighting the growing demand for tertiary education in Nigeria. The registration period, which occurred between January 26 and February 28, 2026, was successfully managed despite the logistical challenges of catering to such a vast volume of students.

As the results have been released and the policy meeting for admission guidelines has been concluded, the focus now shifts entirely to the admission process. Candidates who participated in the April examinations have already begun accessing their results via the SMS service provided by JAMB, specifically by texting 'UTME RESULT' to 55019 or 66019. The Board has reiterated that the integrity of the examination remains a top priority, and despite isolated reports of technical hitches at certain centres, the overall exercise was deemed successful by the regulatory body.

For the 2026/2027 session, competition for placement in federal universities is expected to be stiff. Students are advised to monitor the Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS) daily. CAPS provides a transparent window where institutions upload their admission lists. If you find your name on a school’s list, it is imperative to 'Accept' or 'Reject' the offer on the portal immediately. Delaying the acceptance of a legitimate offer could lead to the candidate’s slot being offered to another qualified applicant. The admission cycle typically spans from June through September 2026, though specific institutions may adjust their local timelines. Staying informed through the official JAMB website and the chosen institution's portal is the best way to secure your place in the upcoming academic session.

excerpt: The 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination has officially concluded across all centers in Nigeria.
fullContent:
The 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) has officially come to a close after a rigorous examination window. Over 2.2 million candidates participated in the Computer-Based Test (CBT) across 989 designated centers nationwide. The board has successfully processed the majority of results, with the first batch of 632,788 results released as early as April. 

As the exercise draws to a close, the focus for the board now shifts toward the Central Admission Processing System (CAPS). Candidates are reminded that their results can be accessed through the official SMS service by sending 'RESULT' to 55019 or 66019 using the phone number registered during the initial application. 

With the examination phase completed, the next phase involves the release of institutional Post-UTME guidelines. Institutions are expected to start publishing their specific requirements and application portals soon. Candidates are encouraged to monitor their profiles on the JAMB portal frequently to ensure all details are correct and to track any changes in admission status. It is crucial that candidates do not engage in any 'admission racketeering' as the board has reiterated its commitment to transparency throughout the 2026/2027 cycle.
title: JAMB Concludes 2026 UTME Nationwide

This development, confirmed by the board on May 15, 2026, allows candidates who have already sat for their examinations to make necessary adjustments to their preferred institutions or course of study to better align with their scores or personal interests.

title: JAMB Announces Major Update on UTME 2026 Change of Institution

This service is now available to allow students to adjust their preferred course or institution choices, provided they meet the specific requirements set by the receiving institutions.

title: New JAMB Directive: UTME Exemption for Specific Agricultural and Education Courses
fullContent:
In a strategic initiative to boost the national workforce in critical sectors, JAMB has announced a new policy for the 2026 admission season. Candidates applying for specific programs in Education and non-Engineering Agricultural fields are now subject to a relaxed admission framework. The policy, aimed at increasing enrollment in these essential disciplines, allows for a more flexible entry process compared to high-demand competitive courses.

This policy change follows deliberations at the 2026 policy meeting, highlighting the federal government's desire to incentivize students towards pedagogical and agrarian studies. While these candidates are still required to register with JAMB, the shift signals a broader move to fill capacity gaps in Nigerian colleges and universities. 

Prospective students who have already registered for the 2026 UTME and are interested in these fields should contact their target institutions to verify if they are eligible for this special consideration. Admissions officers at various universities are expected to integrate these guidelines into their existing criteria. Candidates are encouraged to check the official JAMB portal frequently for detailed lists of courses covered under this exemption policy as institutions begin their post-UTME screening processes.

excerpt: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has announced the removal of the UTME requirement for candidates seeking admission into Colleges of Education (COEs) for Education and Agriculture non-engineering programmes. Starting from the 2027 academic session, candidates will only need four credits in the SSCE to qualify for admission.
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has announced a significant policy change that will impact the admission process for Colleges of Education (COEs) in Nigeria. Effective from the 2027 academic session, JAMB has removed the UTME requirement for candidates seeking admission into COEs for Education and Agriculture non-engineering programmes.

**Key Policy Changes:**

1. **UTME Exemption:** Candidates for Colleges of Education will no longer be required to sit for the UTME for Education and Agriculture non-engineering programmes. Instead, they will only need four credits in the Senior School Certificate Examination (SSCE).

2. **Registration Requirement:** Candidates must still register with JAMB, and their credentials will be screened, verified, and certified for the issuance of admission letters through the Central Admission Processing System (CAPS) in accordance with extant regulations.

**Rationale for the Policy Change:**

The policy change aims to expand access to higher education by reducing barriers for candidates interested in pursuing careers in education and agriculture. By removing the UTME requirement, JAMB seeks to encourage more candidates to consider these fields, which are critical to national development.

**Implementation Timeline:**

The policy takes effect in the 2027 academic session. Candidates are advised to prepare accordingly and ensure they meet the new admission requirements.

**Official Announcement:**

JAMB made the announcement via its official X (formerly Twitter) handle, emphasizing the need for candidates to stay informed about the new admission guidelines.

For further information, candidates are advised to monitor JAMB’s official communications and visit the JAMB website for updates.
title: JAMB Removes UTME Requirement for Education and Agriculture Non-Engineering Courses

excerpt: Candidates who faced technical hitches during the initial 2026 UTME have been granted a second opportunity to sit for the exam.
title: JAMB Schedules June 13 for 2026 UTME Mop-Up Examination
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially fixed Saturday, June 13, 2026, for the conduct of a mop-up examination for affected candidates of the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME). This opportunity is specifically reserved for students who, despite arriving at their designated centers and undergoing biometric verification, were unable to complete their examinations due to unforeseen technical challenges.

The initial examination window, which took place between April 16 and April 29, 2026, saw over 2.2 million candidates participate across 989 centers. While the exercise was largely successful, the board acknowledged that several centers experienced disruptions, leading to the cancellation or postponement of specific sessions. 

Candidates are advised to log into the JAMB portal to confirm their eligibility for the mop-up exercise. Prof. Oloyede emphasized that only candidates who were biometrically verified during the original exercise are eligible for this special sitting. Furthermore, the board continues to investigate cases of examination malpractice from the earlier dates and warns that those involved in irregularities will not be considered for this mop-up window. Students should ensure they monitor their registered email addresses and SMS notifications for updated venue details as the date approaches.

excerpt: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has scheduled a mop-up examination for June 13, 2026, for candidates who encountered technical glitches.
fullContent:
The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially announced that a mop-up examination for the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) will hold on Saturday, June 13, 2026. This decision comes as a relief to thousands of candidates who were unable to complete their examination due to unforeseen technical difficulties encountered at various Computer-Based Test (CBT) centres across the country during the primary examination window, which ran from April 16 to April 29.

According to an official statement, the board has carefully reviewed reports from various examination centres and identified candidates who were biometrically verified but suffered system failures, power outages, or other verified technical setbacks. The mop-up test serves as the final opportunity for these affected students to secure their scores for the 2026 admission cycle.

Candidates are advised to log into their JAMB profiles or visit the official JAMB website to confirm if they have been selected for this mop-up exercise. Furthermore, the board emphasized that only those who were previously verified at their designated centres during the initial attempt are eligible. Failure to appear for this rescheduled examination will result in the loss of the opportunity to participate in the 2026 admission process. The board continues to work with technical partners to ensure the infrastructure for this exercise is robust and capable of handling the remaining candidates efficiently.
title: JAMB Announces June 13 Mop-Up Date for 2026 UTME Candidates

title: Katsina State Launches Initiative to Support Indigent Students
fullContent:
In an effort to ensure that poverty does not become a barrier to academic excellence, the Katsina State government has launched a targeted scholarship and support program for students participating in the 2026/2027 admission exercise. The initiative, dubbed 'Where Poverty Will Not Bury Genius,' is designed to identify high-performing students who have excelled in the UTME but face financial constraints regarding tuition and living expenses.

The project involves a partnership with local universities and polytechnics to provide subsidized fees and direct grants to eligible candidates. Governor Radda emphasized that the state’s human capital is its most valuable asset, and investing in the next generation is the only way to ensure long-term prosperity. The program focuses on STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics) disciplines, which are deemed critical for the state’s industrialization goals.

Interested applicants are encouraged to visit the State Ministry of Education’s portal to submit their applications. Successful candidates will receive stipends to cover books and basic maintenance costs, alleviating the immediate pressure on their families. This initiative reflects a broader trend of state-led interventions to complement federal policies, ensuring that the post-UTME environment is as supportive as possible for every Nigerian student.

Lagos State University has unveiled its 2026/2027 admission screening portal, guiding candidates through a six-step process for UTME and Direct Entry applicants.
fullContent:
## Streamlined Admission Process

Lagos State University (LASU) has launched its official online admission screening platform for the 2026/2027 academic session. The platform aims to simplify the transition for UTME, Direct Entry, and JUPEB candidates into the institution.

## Step-by-Step Screening Guide

According to the university's official portal, the process involves six specific steps: confirming LASU is the first choice on JAMB, verifying entry requirements, logging into the portal with the JAMB number, completing the payment, submitting O-Level and JAMB data, and finally tracking the screening outcome.

## Important Notice

The university explicitly stated: 'Applications from candidates who have not chosen LASU as their first choice will not be processed.' Candidates who have yet to make the switch are urged to visit an accredited JAMB CBT centre immediately to avoid disqualification.

## Student Feedback

Students have praised the clear instructions provided on the portal. One student noted, 'The 6-step guideline on the LASU website is one of the clearest I have seen so far this year.'

## Quick Action Checklist

* Verify LASU is your first choice on the JAMB portal.
* Access the portal via https://services.lidc.lasu.edu.ng.
* Prepare O-Level results and JAMB registration number.
* Use only official payment channels linked on the portal.
* Reach out to screensupport@lasu.edu.ng for technical enquiries.
title: LASU Opens Official Portal for 2026/2027 Admission Screening

fullContent:
## LASU Opens Admission Screening Portal for Candidates

Lagos State University (LASU), Ojo, has officially activated its online admission screening portal for the 2026/2027 academic session. This development marks the formal start of the university's screening process for prospective undergraduate students who chose LASU as their first-choice institution.

The LASU admission process is highly competitive, attracting tens of thousands of applicants yearly. The university's LIDC (Lagos State University Information and Development Centre) has configured the portal to ensure a seamless screening journey for both UTME and Direct Entry candidates.

## Selecting Candidate Category and Eligibility

The LASU screening portal requires candidates to select their specific candidate type—either regular UTME, Direct Entry, or other specialized streams. Each candidate type is associated with distinct academic eligibility criteria. For instance, regular UTME candidates must meet the minimum cutoff mark set for their desired courses, while Direct Entry candidates must provide verified diplomas or NCE credentials.

LASU utilizes a point-grading system for its screening, which evaluates the candidate's UTME score alongside their O'level results. High grades in core subjects like Mathematics, English, and the relevant sciences or arts subjects are crucial for securing a spot.

## Technical Precautions During Registration

Candidates are advised to use stable internet connections when filling out the screening forms. The university cautioned against entering false grades, as any candidate caught with falsified results will be expelled, even if the error is discovered after admission has been granted.

## Quick Action Checklist
- [ ] Access the official LASU online admission portal at services.lidc.lasu.edu.ng.
- [ ] Choose your precise candidate type (UTME or Direct Entry).
- [ ] Input your JAMB registration number and surname to login.
- [ ] Pay the mandatory screening fee via the designated online payment gateway.
- [ ] Carefully input your O'level grades (grades A1 to C6).
- [ ] Verify that your O'level results are fully uploaded to the JAMB CAPS portal.
- [ ] Print out the LASU screening report slip as proof of successful registration.
