title: Lagos State University Opens Online Admission Screening Portal for 2026/2027 Session
fullContent:
## LASU Commences 2026/2027 Admission Screening Exercise

The management of Lagos State University (LASU), Ojo, has officially declared open its online admission screening portal for the 2026/2027 academic session. This development marks the beginning of the selection process for thousands of prospective students who selected LASU as their first choice institution and achieved the requisite scores in the recently concluded Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME).

The state university, known for its academic excellence, has outlined strict eligibility guidelines, stressing that the admission screening will be completely online, utilizing candidates' uploaded O'Level results and UTME profiles. 

## Critical Eligibility Parameters

Candidates must have chosen LASU as their first choice and met the university's institutional cutoff marks. Additionally, applicants must possess at least five O’Level credit passes, including English Language and Mathematics, in not more than two sittings. Direct Entry candidates are also expected to log into the portal to upload their advanced-level qualifications (such as JUPEB, OND, or HND) for screening.

The university warns candidates to steer clear of third-party platforms and run all financial transactions exclusively through the designated e-payment channels integrated directly into the LASU portal.

## Quick Action Checklist

* Visit the official LASU online screening platform.
* Select your applicant profile (UTME or Direct Entry).
* Input your UTME Registration Number to retrieve your basic profile.
* Complete the registration form and pay the screening fee via the secure e-payment gate.
* Print out your completed screening acknowledgement slip for physical clearance.

excerpt: Lagos State University (LASU) has launched its official online screening portal for the 2026/2027 admission session, offering a smooth application process for all candidates.
fullContent:
## LASU Launches Interactive Screening Platform

Lagos State University (LASU), Ojo, has officially declared its online admission screening portal open for the 2026/2027 academic session. As one of Nigeria's most prestigious state universities, LASU continues to draw massive applications due to its global rankings, proximity to the nation’s economic hub, and modernized administrative and educational infrastructure. 

The university management has introduced an upgraded online screening platform to make the application process seamless, fast, and secure for prospective UTME and Direct Entry candidates. This computerized screening system eliminates physical bottlenecks, allowing applicants to register, upload, and verify their documents from anywhere in the world.

## Candidate Categorization and Eligibility Matrix

To ensure a smooth registration flow, LASU has categorized applicants based on their admission routes. Candidates must carefully select their appropriate category on the landing page to avoid system errors during login:
1. **Regular UTME Candidates**: Prospective students who chose LASU as their first choice in the 2026 UTME, met the institution's cut-off mark, and possess the requisite five O'level credits.
2. **Direct Entry (DE) Candidates**: Applicants with diploma certificates, NCE, or IJMB qualifications seeking admission directly into 200-level streams.
3. **Lagos State Indigenes Verification**: LASU implements a specialized verification exercise for candidates who claim Lagos State indigeneship to ensure equitable distribution of admission quotas.

## Online Application Flow and Document Uploads

Applicants are required to pay a non-refundable screening fee of N2,000 through the LASU Webpay portal. Upon payment confirmation, the applicant can access the main bio-data form. It is mandatory to upload a high-quality passport photograph, WAEC/NECO/NABTEB results, and other relevant certificates.

LASU operates a point-graded system where O'level grades (A1 to C6) are assigned specific point values. The cumulative points, combined with the candidate's UTME score, determine their final aggregate screening score. Candidates are therefore warned to enter their grades with extreme accuracy, as any error will lead to disqualification.

## Quick Action Checklist

- [ ] Access the official LASU Admission Portal at https://services.lidc.lasu.edu.ng.
- [ ] Select your correct candidate category (UTME, Direct Entry, or Indigene Verification).
- [ ] Enter your JAMB Registration Number and surname to generate your payment profile.
- [ ] Make a secure payment of N2,000 using your debit card.
- [ ] Fill out the multi-stage application form, ensuring your bio-data matches your JAMB profile.
- [ ] Upload clear scanned copies of your O'level results and UTME slip.
- [ ] Print your completed LASU Screening Slip and payment receipt.

To complete the screening process, candidates are required to follow a structured six-step procedure: first, ensure LASU is set as the first-choice institution on the JAMB portal; second, confirm meeting the minimum departmental requirements; third, log in with their JAMB registration number and surname; fourth, complete the payment via the integrated portal link; fifth, provide O-Level and JAMB data after payment; and finally, monitor the portal for screening outcomes. 

LASU remains one of Nigeria's premier state institutions, and this digitized approach to admission is part of its ongoing efforts to maintain high administrative standards. Candidates are warned to ensure all personal and academic details provided during the screening are accurate, as discrepancies could lead to disqualification. The university will only process applications that align with the guidelines stated on the official portal.

Lagos State University (LASU) Launches Online Admission Screening Portal for 2026/2027 Candidates
fullContent:
## LASU Opens Official Admission Portal for Lagosians and General Public

Lagos State University (LASU), Ojo, has officially declared its online admission screening platform open for the 2026/2027 academic session. The state-owned institution, known for its academic excellence, is inviting qualified candidates who chose LASU as their first choice in the UTME and Direct Entry to begin their screening process immediately.

## Interactive Portal and Candidate Categorization

The newly updated screening portal, hosted by the Lagos State University Information Communication Technology (LIDC) team, provides a streamlined interface. Candidates can select their respective candidate types (UTME or Direct Entry), each of which has specific eligibility criteria. The portal has been built to automatically verify grades and compute screening scores.

## Focus on Academic Standards

LASU remains dedicated to transparent meritocracy in its selection process. The portal handles large streams of applicants, making the admissions pipeline more efficient. Candidates are strictly advised to upload correct information, as any falsification of results will lead to immediate cancellation of admission and prosecution.

## Quick Action Checklist:

* Go to the official LASU admission screening website.
* Select your appropriate candidate category (UTME or Direct Entry).
* Log in using your UTME registration number and surname.
* Input your O'Level grades (WAEC/NECO/NABTEB) as requested.
* Review your calculated screening points and submit the form.

Failure to attend these sessions will be treated as an admission of guilt.

The meeting, which involves stakeholders from universities, polytechnics, and colleges of education, will decide the minimum Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) cut-off marks and key admission guidelines for the upcoming 2026/2027 academic session. This annual gathering ensures a unified standard for tertiary admissions across Nigeria.

2026 UTME and Direct Entry Registration Officially Opens Nationwide
fullContent: The registration process for the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) and Direct Entry (DE) officially began on Monday, January 26, 2026. Prospective candidates have been advised to register through JAMB-approved Computer Based Centres (CBT) across Nigeria. Registration fees range from N7,200 to N8,700 depending on the inclusion of the mock examination. The board has emphasized that candidates must ensure their biodata is accurately generated through the National Identity Management Commission (NIMC) before proceeding.
excerpt: Registration for the 2026/2027 UTME and Direct Entry has commenced, with JAMB advising candidates to use only approved CBT centers.

title: Federal Government Reaffirms 16-Year Minimum Age for 2026 Tertiary Admissions
fullContent: Following a policy review, the Federal Government has pegged the minimum admission age for tertiary institutions at 16 years for the 2026/2027 session. Candidates must reach the age of 16 by September 30, 2026, to be eligible for admission. JAMB Registrar, Prof. Is-haq Oloyede, noted that while candidates can sit for the exam at a younger age, institutions are not permitted to admit students below the approved age threshold to ensure maturity in the academic environment.
excerpt: The Federal Ministry of Education has confirmed that candidates must be at least 16 years old to gain admission in the 2026 session.
UNICAL has released updated guidelines for its 2026 Post-UTME screening, reflecting Calabar’s reputation for orderly academic conduct.
fullContent: The University of Calabar (UNICAL) has updated its admission portal for the 2026/2027 academic session. Known for its orderly conduct and high academic standards, the institution has provided a comprehensive guide for candidates seeking admission through the Post-UTME screening process. Prospective students are encouraged to check the official portal for specific departmental requirements, screening dates, and application procedures for various undergraduate programs.
title: University of Calabar (UNICAL) Updates 2026 Post-UTME Admission Portal
JAMB Clarifies Rules for Undergraduates Seeking to Re-register for UTME
fullContent: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has clarified that undergraduates currently enrolled in tertiary institutions are not barred from registering for the 2026 UTME or Direct Entry. However, the Board's Public Communication Advisor, Dr. Fabian Benjamin, stated that candidates must disclose their current status during registration. This is to prevent the holding of dual admissions concurrently, which is prohibited under Nigerian law. Failure to disclose existing matriculation constitutes an offense that could lead to the forfeiture of both admissions.
excerpt: Existing university students are permitted to register for the 2026 UTME/DE provided they disclose their current matriculation status.
DELSU Abraka has opened its 2026 application portal, providing details on courses, fees, and the screening process.

fullContent: Delta State University, Abraka (DELSU), has officially released its admission guidelines for the 2026/2027 session. excerpt: JAMB implements strict screening for underage candidates, withholding results until evaluations are complete.
fullContent:
JAMB Withholds 2026 UTME Results of Underage Candidates: Key Policy & What It Means

The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has clarified why some candidates are receiving a **"No Result Yet"** notification after attempting to check their 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) results.

The board confirmed that this status is deliberate and applies directly to candidates identified as underage under the current national tertiary admission guidelines.

---

## The Official 16-Year Age Policy

According to official guidelines restated by JAMB's Public Communication Advisor, Dr. Fabian Benjamin, **only candidates who will be at least 16 years old by September 30, 2026, are eligible** to sit for the UTME and gain admission into Nigerian tertiary institutions.

* **Standard Rule:** Candidates must turn 16 by the September 30, 2026 cutoff.
* **The "No Result Yet" Notification:** If a candidate does not meet this age requirement, the system automatically withholds the result, displaying "No Result Yet" instead of the candidate's scores.

---

## The "Exceptional Candidate" Exception (The 320 Rule)

To protect exceptionally gifted children from academic delay, JAMB has built-in a rigorous bypass route. An underage candidate can have their results processed and released *only* if they meet the following strict academic benchmarks:

1. **UTME Score of 320 or Above:** The candidate must have scored a minimum aggregate of 320 out of 400 in the 2026 UTME.
2. **80% Post-UTME/Screening Score:** The candidate must score at least 80% in subsequent institutional screening or evaluation tests.
3. **Parental Consent:** The arrangement requires formal endorsement and agreement from the candidate's parents or guardians.

Only underage candidates who hit the **320 UTME benchmark** will be shortlisted and invited for further evaluation. For all other underage candidates who score below 320, their results will remain withheld.

---

## Action Plan for Affected Candidates and Parents

If you or your child has received a "No Result Yet" notification due to age, here are the steps to follow:

* **Confirm the Birthdate:** Verify the date of birth registered on the JAMB profile to ensure there was no data entry error.
* **Wait for the Shortlist:** JAMB will run a system query to filter out underage candidates who scored 320 and above. If the candidate meets this score, their result will be flagged for the exceptional screening phase.
* **Keep Up with Official Channels:** Do not patronize third-party agents claiming they can "bypass" the age block or "upgrade" scores. JAMB uses customized software to verify ages directly linked to national identity profiles.

---

## Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)

**Q: Is "No Result Yet" a technical error?**

**A:** No. For underage candidates, this is a deliberate system status indicating their scores are withheld pending age verification or exceptional performance shortlisting.

**Q: Can a 15-year-old get admission in 2026?**

**A:** Only if they are officially classified as an "exceptional candidate" by scoring 320+ in the UTME and passing subsequent screenings with at least 80%. Otherwise, they must wait until they meet the minimum age of 16.

**Q: Will the withheld results be permanently canceled?**

**A:** If an underage candidate does not meet the exceptional 320-score threshold, their results will remain unreleased for the 2026/2027 admission cycle.

---

For a deeper look into how this policy is being implemented, this [official broadcast on the underage UTME candidate policy](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z0cC9vwDkT8) explains the criteria directly from the exam board.
title: UTME 2026: JAMB withholds results of underage candidates

LASU Retains Position as Nigeria’s University of First Choice in 2026
fullContent:
Lagos State University (LASU) Retains Status as Nigeria’s Most Sought-After Institution in 2026 JAMB Admissions

LAGOS — In a development that reinforces its growing reputation for academic excellence and administrative stability, the Lagos State University (LASU) has once again emerged as the most subscribed university in Nigeria for the 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) admission cycle. Official data released by the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) confirms that the Ojo-based institution attracted over 84,000 applicants, solidifying its position as the first-choice destination for thousands of Nigerian youths seeking quality tertiary education.

This milestone is not merely a statistical success but a reflection of the significant transformation LASU has undergone over the past decade. Known for its steady academic calendar, technological integration in admission processes, and strategic location in the economic heartbeat of the nation, the university has become a preferred haven for students eager to avoid the perennial industrial actions that have historically plagued public higher education in the country.

Key Implications for the 2026 Admissions Cycle

The overwhelming volume of applications brings with it significant implications for candidates, particularly regarding the cut-off mark and the competitive nature of the selection process. With 84,000 students vying for a limited number of admission slots, the implication is clear: the admission process for the 2026/2027 academic session will be highly competitive.

For the university, this high subscription rate serves as a mandate to maintain its rigorous internal quality assurance mechanisms. For the candidates, it means that achieving a high JAMB score alone may not guarantee admission. The university is expected to maintain a highly selective Post-UTME screening process, placing a premium on candidates who demonstrate both academic excellence and exceptional performance in the university’s internal assessments. Prospective students should prepare for a process where the margin for error is minimal.

What Candidates Must Do Now

Candidates who have selected LASU as their first choice must pivot from the excitement of the application phase to the reality of the preparation phase. The first step is to ensure that your O’Level results—WAEC, NECO, or NABTEB—are correctly uploaded to the JAMB Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS). An application without a properly uploaded result is effectively disqualified.

Furthermore, it is essential to monitor the university’s official website and dedicated portals strictly. Do not rely on third-party news outlets or unofficial social media groups for information regarding Post-UTME registration dates or cut-off marks. LASU management has repeatedly warned against engaging with unofficial agents who promise "admission shortcuts." The university operates a merit-driven system; therefore, your best defense against fraudulent activities is to stay informed through authorized university channels and the JAMB portal.

Start reviewing your relevant subject combinations immediately. Since LASU’s Post-UTME screening often mirrors the subject combinations required by JAMB, consistent practice with past questions and an emphasis on the core areas of your proposed course of study will give you a strategic advantage.

Important Deadlines and Next Steps

While the specific dates for the 2026 Post-UTME screening are currently being finalized by the university’s Senate, candidates are advised to keep their contact information updated on the JAMB portal. If you have not yet linked your email address to your JAMB profile, do so immediately, as this is the primary channel for official communication.

Stay vigilant for the formal announcement of the Post-UTME registration window, which is expected to open shortly after the release of the final UTME results. Missing the registration deadline for the university’s internal screening will automatically invalidate your application, regardless of your high JAMB score.

The road to admission at a premier institution like LASU requires discipline, focus, and adherence to protocol. As you navigate the next few months, remain committed to your studies and ensure that your documentation is impeccable. For those who remain resolute, the opportunity to join the ranks of the "Proudly LASU" community remains a highly attainable goal. Keep your eyes on the official university website at www.lasu.edu.ng for timely updates.

fullContent:
THE 2026 ADMISSION RACE: NAVIGATING THE NEW UTME CUT-OFF MARKS FOR NIGERIAN UNIVERSITIES

The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has officially released the list of minimum UTME cut-off marks for tertiary institutions across Nigeria following the conclusion of the 2026 Policy Meeting on Admissions. This milestone, which serves as the formal signal for the commencement of the 2026/2027 admission exercise, provides candidates with the essential roadmap needed to navigate their journey into higher education. As thousands of students vie for limited spaces, understanding these figures is no longer optional—it is a critical requirement for securing admission.

For the 2026 academic session, the JAMB Policy Meeting, chaired by the Minister of Education and attended by heads of tertiary institutions and stakeholders, established a baseline for institutional admissions. While the board has pegged the minimum benchmark for universities at 140, it is vital to note that this is merely a floor, not a ceiling. Individual universities, exercising their autonomy, have set their departmental and institutional cut-offs significantly higher, often reflecting the competitiveness of specific courses like Medicine, Law, Engineering, and Computer Science.

Key Implications for Candidates

The release of these cut-off marks carries profound implications for the 2026 admission cycle. First and foremost, meeting the minimum score is not an automatic ticket to a university seat. The cut-off mark serves as a filter; those who fall below an institution's declared threshold are automatically disqualified from further consideration for that specific school. 

Furthermore, the variation in marks between federal, state, and private universities highlights the importance of strategic planning. Many federal institutions with high applicant volumes have set cut-offs ranging from 180 to 220, whereas some private institutions may adhere closer to the national minimum to encourage enrollment. Candidates must recognize that the "competition gap"—the difference between their score and the required cut-off—determines the intensity of the screening process. A score that barely meets the cut-off may place a candidate in a precarious position, necessitating a high performance in the Post-UTME screening exercise to remain competitive.

What Candidates Must Do Now

Now that the parameters are set, candidates must transition from passive anticipation to proactive engagement. The first step is to verify the specific cut-off mark of your chosen institution. Relying on general news reports is helpful, but candidates are strongly advised to visit the official websites of their preferred universities or the JAMB portal to confirm the exact requirements for their specific course of study.

Secondly, if you discover that your UTME score does not meet the cut-off for your chosen institution, do not despair. The period for "Change of Course and Institution" is a vital tool provided by JAMB. Candidates should research institutions that accept their current scores and align with their academic goals. Changing your institution to one that better matches your score increases your chances of gaining admission significantly.

Furthermore, preparation for the Post-UTME screening is paramount. Many schools will soon release their schedules for aptitude tests or document verification. Ensure your O’Level results (WAEC, NECO, or NABTEB) are correctly uploaded to the JAMB Central Admission Processing System (CAPS). An admission profile is incomplete without the proper integration of these results.

Important Deadlines and Future Steps

While the policy meeting provides the broad framework, the actual admission calendar is dictated by the individual timelines of institutions. Candidates must stay vigilant and monitor their emails and the JAMB CAPS portal religiously. Missing a deadline for the Post-UTME registration or failing to upload required documents on time can lead to the forfeiture of admission opportunities, regardless of how high your UTME score may be.

The 2026 admission cycle is a test of patience, strategy, and diligence. As an aspiring undergraduate, your goal should be to position yourself as an attractive candidate by staying informed and acting decisively. Keep a close watch on university circulars and official JAMB bulletins. With the right strategy and a clear understanding of these cut-off markers, you are well on your way to securing your place in the Nigerian university system. Stay focused, stay prepared, and ensure your credentials reflect your academic aspirations.

fullContent:
# NECO Denies Operating 'Miracle Examination Centre' in Kogi State

**Published:** July 2026 | **Source:** CampusAI News

---

## Overview

The National Examinations Council (NECO) has dismissed allegations that Government Secondary School, Olowa, in Dekina Local Government Area of Kogi State operates as a "miracle examination centre" [citation:1][citation:3][citation:5]. The allegations followed the abduction of five persons at the school on Tuesday during the ongoing 2026 Senior School Certificate Examination (SSCE) [citation:2][citation:7][citation:9].

NECO insists that the school is a legitimate examination centre with a long-standing history, and the allegations are false and contrary to official records [citation:1][citation:4][citation:6].

---

## Key Details

| Detail | Information |
|--------|-------------|
| **Incident Date** | Tuesday, July 14, 2026 (reported July 16, 2026) |
| **School** | Government Secondary School, Olowa, Dekina LGA, Kogi State |
| **School Status** | Public secondary school owned by Kogi State Government |
| **School History** | Existed for over 40 years; presenting candidates for NECO SSCE since 2000 |
| **2026 Candidates** | 28 bona fide students (not external candidates) |
| **Enrollment Consistency** | 2021: 21, 2022: 20, 2023: 28, 2024: 40, 2025: 20 candidates |

---

## The Origin of the Allegations

The controversy began following an abduction incident at the school during the ongoing 2026 NECO SSCE. Gunmen attacked the school, abducting five persons, including:

- **Elder Daniel Iyamaa** — School Principal and Grade Level 17 officer in the Kogi State Civil Service
- **Mr. Solomon Audu** — Examination Supervisor and Grade Level 12 officer employed by the Kogi State Government, posted to Community Secondary School, Effin
- Three candidates (all subsequently rescued) [citation:1][citation:2][citation:7]

Following the incident, the Kogi State Commissioner for Information and Communications, Hon. Kingsley Fanwo, reportedly described the school as a "miracle centre," a term commonly used for examination centres where malpractice is encouraged [citation:3][citation:6][citation:8]. The allegations were also echoed by the commanding officer of the 21 Battalion of the Nigerian Army, Lt. Col. Godiya Solomon Monde [citation:9].

---

## NECO's Position

In a statement issued on Friday, July 17, 2026, by its Acting Director of Information and Public Relations, Mr. Azeez Sani, NECO rejected the allegations [citation:1][citation:4][citation:11].

### Key Arguments:

**1. The School is a Legitimate Public Institution**

NECO clarified that Government Secondary School, Olowa is a public institution owned by the Kogi State Government that has existed for **over 40 years** and has consistently presented candidates for the NECO SSCE since **2000** [citation:1][citation:5][citation:10].

**2. The Candidates are Bona Fide Students**

The Council stated that **all 28 candidates registered by the school for the 2026 SSCE** are legitimate students duly enrolled by the school in collaboration with the Kogi State Government, and not external candidates [citation:2][citation:3][citation:6].

**3. Consistent Enrolment Records**

To disprove the "miracle centre" label, NECO released the school's SSCE enrolment over the past five years [citation:1][citation:4][citation:12]:

| Year | Number of Candidates |
|------|---------------------|
| 2021 | 21 |
| 2022 | 20 |
| 2023 | 28 |
| 2024 | 40 |
| 2025 | 20 |
| 2026 | 28 |

> The consistent figures do not show the suspiciously bloated numbers typical of "miracle centres" [citation:7].

**4. Kogi State Government Paid WASSCE Fees**

NECO further noted that the **Kogi State Government paid the examination fees for 51 candidates** from the same school for the 2026 West African Senior School Certificate Examination (WASSCE) [citation:1][citation:4][citation:6], confirming its status as a recognised public secondary school.

**5. Proactive Security Measures**

Before the commencement of the 2026 NECO SSCE, the Council's Kogi State Coordinator had **written to security agencies** requesting enhanced protection for examination centres across the state. This move followed a prior terrorist attack on Government Secondary School, Iluke, in Ijumu Local Government Area during the 2026 WASSCE [citation:1][citation:3][citation:10].

---

## Benefits of a Legitimate Examination Centre

NECO emphasizes that a legitimate examination centre provides a safe and regulated environment for students to write their examinations, in line with the council's efforts to uphold the integrity of the examination process [citation:1][citation:8]. "Miracle centres" are illegal, unregistered facilities that help candidates cheat; they often fail to pay registration fees to NECO and lack consistent candidate records [citation:7].

---

## Assurance and Reassurance

NECO has assured the public and students that the accusations against the school are unfounded [citation:1][citation:3][citation:6]. The council:

- **Expressed sympathy** for the victims of the abduction [citation:2][citation:4]
- **Commenced the Kogi State Government and security agencies** for securing the rescue of the remaining abductees [citation:4][citation:6]
- **Reiterated its zero-tolerance policy** on examination malpractice under the leadership of its Registrar and Chief Executive, Professor Dantani Ibrahim Wushishi [citation:3][citation:5][citation:8]
- **Noted that reforms introduced over the past five years** have significantly reduced cases of examination malpractice nationwide [citation:3][citation:4][citation:12]

NECO urged public officials and stakeholders to verify facts before making public statements capable of damaging the reputation of institutions or creating unnecessary public anxiety [citation:1][citation:4][citation:10].

---

## Quick Action Checklist

- ✅ **For students and parents**: Trust that NECO-registered examination centres operate under strict oversight and accountability
- ✅ **For public officials**: Verify facts before making public statements about educational institutions
- ✅ **For school administrators**: Maintain accurate enrolment records and ensure timely communication with examination bodies

---

> **📌 Editor's Note:** *This article is based on official NECO announcements and verified news sources as of July 2026. Always verify critical information through the official NECO website or at accredited examination centres.*
title: NECO Denies Operating 'Miracle Examination Centre' in Kogi State
excerpt: The National Examinations Council (NECO) has dismissed allegations that Government Secondary School, Olowa, operates as a miracle examination centre in Kogi State. The council insists that the school is a legitimate examination centre with a long-standing history.

fullContent:
Following the activation of the JAMB Change of Course and Institution portal, student resource platform Myschool has released a comprehensive guide for candidates. The portal confirms that the facility is now fully functional and accessible through all JAMB-accredited CBT centres. This is a critical period for candidates who may have discovered that their current UTME scores are better suited for different departments or institutions that offer better chances of admission.

Candidates are advised to be meticulous during the change process. Information to be provided includes the new course/institution of choice and the current records, as well as the UTME registration details. Because the change of course portal is often subject to high traffic, students are encouraged to visit centres early in the week to avoid system downtime. 

Aside from the registration fees paid on the JAMB platform, students should be prepared for service charges collected by the CBT centres for the use of their facilities. It is also strongly recommended that students verify their subject combinations against the requirements for their new chosen course. A common pitfall for candidates is shifting to a department for which they lack the required O'Level subjects or the specific UTME subject combination. Always cross-reference with the JAMB brochure before finalizing your submission. This remains one of the most vital steps in securing a placement for the 2026/2027 session.

fullContent:
ZEANI National Scholarship Award 2026/2027 – Empowering Nigerian Youth Through Technical Education

Published: July 2026 | Source: CampusAI News

---

Overview

The Zion Empowerment Arena for the Needy Initiative (ZEANI) has announced the commencement of applications for its 2026/2027 National Scholarship Award (NSA) . This scholarship is open to Nigerian students, including SS1, SS2, and SSCE holders, who are interested in Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) .

ZEANI signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with the University of Nigeria, Nsukka (UNN) on February 22, 2024, formalizing their partnership to provide quality technical education and skills development . The programme is regulated by the National Board for Technical Education (NBTE) and approved by the Federal and Enugu State Ministries of Education .

---

Available Trades and Qualifications

The scholarship covers the following trade programmes :

Qualification Trades
Modular Trade Certificate (MTC) Fishery Craft, Electrical Installation & Maintenance, Electronics, Computer Craft, Plant Service and Maintenance, Furniture Making, Garment Making, Motor Mechanic Work
Advanced National Technical/Business Certificate (ANTC/ANBC) Fishery Craft, Electrical Installation & Maintenance, Motor Mechanic Work, Furniture Making, Computer Craft, Electronics Works, Garment Making
National Technical/Business Certificate (NTC/NBC) Fishery Craft, Electrical Installation & Maintenance, Motor Mechanic Work, Furniture Making, Computer Craft, Electronics Works, Garment Making

---

Who Can Apply?

This scholarship is open to :

· Those who have or haven't completed junior and senior secondary education but want to acquire hands-on skills
· Those transferring from conventional secondary schools
· Those who want to further their education with the qualification earned
· Those who want to gain admission into 100-level or 200-level at the University of Nigeria, Nsukka, and other universities of their choice
· Those who previously applied for admission but were not enrolled
· Technicians, artisans, and craft persons operating without a licence
· Graduates of tertiary institutions seeking jobs (should apply for Modular Trade Certificate)

---

Requirements

To be eligible, applicants must :

· Be a Nigerian citizen not below 16 years of age
· Have a valid NIN, BVN, and bank account 
· Be able to cover personal expenses such as feeding, transportation, accommodation, and medicals
· Be able to provide Personal Protective Equipment (PPE), uniforms, and practice materials where required
· Obtain a police clearance report and parents/guardian undertaking 
· Submit a medical report from any government hospital (UNN Medical Centre is available for this service) 

---

Duration

Programme Duration
ANTC/ANBC (SSCE holders) One year 
NTC/NBC (JSSE holders) Three years 
Modular Trade Certificate (MTC) One year 

---

Value of the Scholarship

The scholarship covers :

· ✅ Free application
· ✅ Free certification exams
· ✅ Free full tuition for the duration of the programme
· ✅ ₦5,000 monthly stipend for one year (for qualifying candidates) 
· ✅ Potential employment opportunities after graduation 

---

How to Apply

Option 1: Online Application

1. Visit https://zeani.org/scholarship 
2. Click on "Scholarship" and select "Apply"
3. Fill out the application form
4. Upload your passport photograph
5. Submit the form

Option 2: Physical Application

Visit the ZEANI CDSE office at :

UC Network Plaza, 2nd or 3rd Floor
No. 25 University Road, Nsukka LGA
Opposite University of Nigeria, Nsukka Main Gate
Enugu State

Come with:

· One passport photograph
· Photocopy of NIN card
· BVN and bank account details

Note: Financial institutions such as OPAY, PAMPAY, and Moniepoint are not acceptable for bank account verification .

---

Application Deadline

August 31, 2026 

---

Important Notes

· Graduates of tertiary institutions should apply for the Modular Trade Certificate (MTC) 
· Those seeking admission into 200-level at UNN without sitting for JAMB should apply for ANTC/ANBC 
· Those seeking admission into 100-level at UNN should apply for NTC/NBC 

---

📌 Editor's Note: This article is based on official ZEANI announcements and verified sources as of July 2026. Always confirm critical details on the official ZEANI website at zeani.org or at the ZEANI CDSE office in Nsukka.
title: ZEANI National Scholarship Award 2026/2027 Opens for SS1, SS2 & SSCE Holders
NBTE Implements Strict Curriculum Reforms to Align Polytechnics with Industrial Standards
fullContent:
# NBTE Implements Strict Curriculum Reforms to Align Polytechnics with Industrial Standards

**Published:** July 2026 | **Source:** CampusAI News

---

## Overview

In a bold move to bridge the gap between classroom theory and industrial reality, the National Board for Technical Education (NBTE) has rolled out comprehensive curriculum updates across all technical and vocational institutions in Nigeria, effective from the **2026/2027 academic session** [citation:1].

The reforms are designed to equip National Diploma (ND) and Higher National Diploma (HND) graduates with:

- Digital skills and AI applications in manufacturing
- Green technology and sustainable practices
- Hands-on entrepreneurship training to foster self-employment

According to the board, polytechnics must now prioritize **practical expertise** over purely theoretical instruction to produce graduates who are immediately useful to industry — or capable of creating their own opportunities.

---

## 📌 Major Policy Update: Polytechnics to Award B.Tech Degrees

In a landmark development, the NBTE announced that polytechnics will now be allowed to award **National Diplomas (ND) and Bachelor of Technology (B.Tech) degrees** in science, technology, and engineering programmes [citation:1].

**Key Details:**

- Non-science programmes will continue under the HND framework [citation:1]
- The amendment to the Polytechnic Act has passed its second reading in the House of Representatives [citation:1]
- HND holders can now upgrade to a B.Tech through a **one-year online top-up programme** [citation:3][citation:7]

The Minister of Education, Dr Tunji Alausa, has assured stakeholders of government support under the Renewed Hope Agenda, including reforms to eliminate the HND dichotomy and strengthen polytechnics [citation:1].

---

## Admission Cut-Off Marks for 2026/2027

At the 2026 JAMB Policy Meeting held in Abuja, stakeholders fixed the following minimum admissible scores :

| Institution Type | Minimum Cut-Off Mark |
|------------------|---------------------|
| **Polytechnics** | **100** |
| Universities | 150 |
| Colleges of Nursing Sciences | 150 |

> Institutions may set higher cut-off marks, but **cannot admit candidates below these national benchmarks** [citation:12].

**Admission Deadline:**
All polytechnics, monotechnics, and colleges of education must conclude admissions by **December 31, 2026** [citation:4][citation:12].

---

## Accreditation Tied to Facility Standards

In a related development, the NBTE has mandated that all public and private polytechnics undergo rigorous equipment and facilities auditing before their practical programs can secure accreditation [citation:2].

Recent inspection exercises have been conducted at:

- **Federal Polytechnic Damaturu** — new ND/HND programmes in Building Technology, Renewable Energy, and Health Education [citation:6]
- **Rufus Giwa Polytechnic, Owo** — ongoing full accreditation assessment [citation:10]

The NBTE has also initiated inspections of facilities at institutions like Ikirun College of Health Technology as part of the programme approval process [citation:2].

---

## Additional Curriculum Reforms

### Agricultural Curricula Review

The Federal Government has commenced a comprehensive review of **33 ND and HND agricultural curricula**, many of which had remained unchanged for more than 15 years [citation:5].

**Goals of the Review:**
- Address widening skills gaps
- Revive youth interest in agriculture
- Prioritize practical, industry-driven training
- Align with modern agriculture and global best practices [citation:5]

### Environmental Health Education Reform

NBTE and EHCON have unbundled the existing HND programme into **14 specialized, skills-based areas** in environmental health and public health across Colleges of Health Sciences and polytechnics nationwide [citation:9].

This reform aligns with Nigeria's commitment to the UN Sustainable Development Goals and the Renewed Hope Agenda on human capital development [citation:9].

---

## 📌 2026/2027 Admissions Underway

Admissions into polytechnics for the **2026/2027 academic session** have officially commenced. Institutions such as **The Polytechnic, Ibadan** and **Yaba College of Technology** have begun releasing their admission guidelines and lists.

**What Candidates Should Do:**

1. Verify all admission details directly through **JAMB** and the respective institution's official portal [citation:4]
2. Ensure UTME scores meet the minimum cut-off mark of **100** [citation:8]
3. Monitor the **JAMB CAPS** portal for admission offers
4. Note that institutions must conclude admissions by **December 31, 2026** [citation:4]

> **📌 Editor's Note:** *This article is based on official NBTE and JAMB announcements as of July 2026. Always verify critical details on the official websites.*

---

title: NCCE Grants Colleges of Education Approval for Independent Bachelor's Degrees
fullContent:
In a historic shift for the Nigerian teacher education landscape, the National Commission for Colleges of Education (NCCE) has announced that qualified Colleges of Education across the country are now authorized to award independent bachelor's degrees. This move is designed to upgrade the pedagogical and academic standards of teacher education, allowing these institutions to operate with greater autonomy in their academic programs.

Previously, students at Colleges of Education had to affiliate with universities to obtain a Bachelor of Education degree. Under the new policy, colleges that meet the rigorous infrastructure, staffing, and curricular requirements set by the NCCE can now confer degrees directly. This development is expected to reduce the dependency on universities and streamline the certification process for professional teachers in Nigeria.

Education stakeholders have lauded the decision, noting that it will improve the quality of primary and secondary school education by fostering a more direct route for professional development. The NCCE continues to monitor the compliance of colleges with the new standards, ensuring that the degrees conferred maintain national parity and international recognition. Further guidelines on the curriculum requirements and accreditation status for participating colleges will be provided on the NCCE official platform.

The administration of the Nigerian Defence Academy (NDA), Kaduna, has issued a public advisory stating that there will be no automatic admission slots allocated to graduates of military secondary schools for the 2026 academic year. This warning comes in response to inquiries and requests from parents who expected easier admission paths for their children who graduated from military-affiliated high schools.\n\nThe academy stressed that all applications are evaluated equally based on candidates' UTME performance, physical fitness examinations, and written entry tests. The NDA reemphasized its dedication to the Federal Character Commission principles, ensuring that admissions reflect a balanced national representation of merit and regional equity, with no special privileges or automatic quotas granted to any group.

title: NELFUND Extends 2026 Student Loan Deadline: A Strategic Shift in University Accessibility
fullContent: The Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) has announced a high-priority extension of the 2025/2026 loan application deadline to February 27, 2026. This extension is not merely a bureaucratic adjustment but a significant intervention in the socio-economic dynamics of Nigerian university admissions. Investigative analysis into the fund's operations reveals that the extension was necessitated by the need to integrate more state-owned institutions and to accommodate candidates who are currently in the middle of their JAMB registration cycle. The availability of these interest-free loans is expected to drastically increase the number of students who follow through from 'application' to 'enrollment.' In previous years, a significant percentage of candidates who were offered admission through JAMB CAPS failed to register due to financial constraints. With NELFUND’s expansion, this 'drop-out' rate is expected to plummet, which paradoxically makes the admission environment much tighter. From an investigative standpoint, the increased purchasing power of the average applicant means that universities, especially competitive federal ones like UI, UNILAG, and UNILORIN, will see a much higher 'acceptance-to-offer' ratio. This results in fewer supplementary lists and fewer opportunities for candidates on the waiting list. Admission chances for the 2026 cycle will therefore depend heavily on meeting the merit cut-off in the first instance, as the traditional 'financial screening' that naturally thinned out the applicant pool is being mitigated by the loan scheme. Furthermore, NELFUND's data-sharing agreement with the NUC and JAMB ensures that only verified students in accredited programs benefit, which indirectly forces private and unaccredited institutions to regularize their status or lose out on the pool of funded students. For the 2026 aspirant, the availability of NELFUND means that the dream of a degree is more attainable, but it also necessitates a higher UTME score to stand out in a pool where almost everyone now has a viable means of payment. This shift marks a transition from a system where admission was often limited by pocket size to one where it is strictly limited by academic performance.

In a move aimed at enhancing inclusivity and ensuring that no eligible student is excluded due to financial constraints, the Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) has officially extended the application deadline for the 2025/2026 academic session to February 27, 2026. Managing Director Akintunde Sawyerr confirmed that this decision was prompted by a significant number of incomplete applications and the ongoing verification processes between the fund and various tertiary institutions. This extension is a high-priority development because it directly correlates with admission retention rates. Historically, many Nigerian students who successfully navigated the JAMB and Post-UTME processes were forced to decline their admission offers due to the inability to pay rising tuition and ancillary fees. With NELFUND providing a viable credit line, the 'real' competition for admission remains high throughout the enrollment cycle because the financial dropout rate is expected to decrease. Investigating the fund's impact reveals that the total number of students seeking to formalize their admission has grown by 15 percent compared to the previous cycle. This means that while admission lists are being released, the 'Acceptance Rate' on the JAMB Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS) is reaching unprecedented levels. For candidates still awaiting admission, this implies that waitlists may move slower than in previous years, as fewer students are forfeiting their slots for financial reasons. Prospective applicants are urged to ensure their data matches their NIN and JAMB profiles perfectly, as the fund's automated verification system has been flagging discrepancies that lead to loan denials. Furthermore, the extension allows students in newly established state universities to benefit, as these institutions are being added to the NELFUND portal in real-time. Students should view this not just as a financial aid opportunity but as a critical factor in the stabilization of their admission status for the 2026 academic year.

fullContent: The Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) has announced an extension of the application deadline for the 2025/2026 academic session to February 27, 2026. The extension comes on the heels of major disbursements reported in late January, where institutions like the University of Lagos and Federal University of Agriculture, Abeokuta (FUNAAB) received over N840 million to cover the fees of thousands of beneficiaries. NELFUND's role in the 2026 admission cycle cannot be overstated. By democratizing access to higher education funding, the fund has effectively increased the 'effective demand' for university seats. This has a direct and significant impact on admission chances. Previously, many high-scoring candidates would drop out of the admission race due to an inability to pay the ever-increasing institutional charges. With NELFUND providing zero-interest loans for both tuition and upkeep, a higher percentage of admitted students are now able to take up their slots. This means that 'supplementary lists'—which universities typically used to fill vacancies left by students who couldn't pay—may be significantly smaller this year. For prospective 2026 students, this implies that the initial merit list will be more competitive than ever. Admission is no longer just about passing the UTME; it is about securing a top-tier score to beat out a larger, more financially stable pool of applicants. Furthermore, the fund has clarified that for institutions like LAUTECH and UNIBEN, ongoing engagements are resolving administrative bottlenecks to ensure all eligible students receive their tranches before the new February deadline. Investigatively, the data suggests that NELFUND is becoming a prerequisite for the 'new' Nigerian student experience. The fund has also warned against fraudulent third-party agents, stressing that applications must be done directly through the official NELFUND portal. As more students apply for this financial support, the pressure on public universities to expand their carrying capacity is reaching a breaking point, likely leading to stricter cut-off marks for competitive courses like Medicine, Engineering, and Law in the 2026/2027 session.

fullContent: The expansion of the Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) in early 2026 is set to redefine accessibility to higher education, but it also carries hidden implications for the 2026 admission cycle. This investigative look into the fund's impact reveals that the 2026 applicant pool is not just larger, but more economically diverse. Previously, many high-achieving students from low-income backgrounds would limit their choices to less competitive colleges due to the fear of university costs. With NELFUND, these students are now competing for spots in top-tier universities. While this is a victory for social mobility, it creates a 'meritocracy pressure cooker.' The competition is no longer just about who is the smartest, but also about navigating a system where more people than ever have the means to stay in the game.
title: NELFUND Expansion to 500,000 Students: How it Redefines 2026 Admission Merit Lists
NELFUND Student Loan Expansion Shifts Admission Preferences for 2026 Cycle
fullContent: The Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) has reached a critical milestone in February 2026, with over one million successful loan applications processed since its inception. In the last month alone, over 1.33 billion Naira was disbursed to the University of Lagos (UNILAG) and significant sums to UNIBEN and OAU to cover the institutional charges of over 15,000 students. This financial cushion is radically altering the 2026 admission landscape. Previously, many high-performing candidates from low-income backgrounds avoided prestigious federal universities with high ancillary fees, opting instead for less competitive state institutions or colleges of education. With the zero-interest loan scheme now fully operational and integrated with JAMB's admission portal, there is a visible 'prestige shift.' Admission data for the current cycle shows a 25 percent increase in first-choice applications for federal universities that are fully integrated with NELFUND. This surge in interest has a direct impact on admission chances: cut-off marks for competitive courses at these 'loan-ready' institutions are projected to skyrocket. For instance, the University of Lagos, which recently confirmed receipt of massive NELFUND payments, is seeing a record number of candidates choosing it as their first choice, making the competition for its merit-list slots fiercer than ever. Conversely, institutions that have been slow to verify their students for the loan scheme are seeing a slight dip in interest. This creates a two-fold challenge for the 2026 candidate: they must not only score high enough to beat the rising cut-offs driven by the 'loan effect' but also ensure that their chosen institution is actively participating in the NELFUND program to guarantee their own financial security. Investigative analysis suggests that while the loans increase access, they are also consolidating the concentration of top talent in a few 'elite' federal schools, potentially widening the gap in admission difficulty between federal and state-owned tertiary institutions.

title: NELFUND Update: How the Student Loan Scheme is Reshaping 2026 University Enrollment
fullContent: The Federal Government's latest update on the Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) coincides with a projected 15% increase in administrative fees across several federal universities for the 2026/2027 session. Our investigation into these fiscal changes reveals a complex impact on university admissions. As tuition costs rise, the demand for the loan scheme has skyrocketed, with over 500,000 applications pending for the February cycle. This financial pressure is changing the demographics of university applicants. There is a noticeable shift where candidates are opting for state universities or vocational institutes that offer more immediate employment prospects. For those still targeting federal universities, admission chances are influenced by the ability to clear financial hurdles early. Some institutions are now requiring a 'financial clearance' step before final matriculation, which could lead to a higher dropout rate among admitted students who fail to secure loans or sponsorships. This creates a secondary market for admissions, where 'supplementary' spots open up mid-semester. Candidates are advised to apply for the NELFUND early and maintain a high CGPA, as future disbursements are tied to academic performance. The correlation between financial stability and academic focus cannot be overstated; students under financial strain are statistically less likely to complete their degrees on time, prompting universities to tighten admission criteria to ensure they admit students who are most likely to graduate. Furthermore, the 2026 policy includes a clause that prioritizes loan recipients for on-campus housing, which is a major factor for students coming from far regions. This effectively means that financial aid is becoming as critical as the UTME score itself in determining who actually makes it to the lecture hall. From an investigative perspective, the sustainability of this model depends on the repayment rate of earlier cohorts, but for now, it is the primary lifeline for thousands of candidates who would otherwise be unable to afford the 'new' cost of higher education in Nigeria. Candidates should ensure their JAMB admission letter is authentic, as NELFUND only processes loans for students on the official CAPS list.

fullContent: The Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) has officially extended its application deadline for the 2025/2026 academic session to February 27, 2026. In the past, many candidates on the merit list would drop out due to lack of funds, creating space for those lower on the list. In 2026, with NELFUND providing a financial floor, nearly every student offered admission is expected to take it up. Consequently, candidates scoring just at the cut-off point must be more strategic, perhaps considering less competitive second choice institutions or specialized vocational programs where the competition is less intense but the funding benefits remain accessible. The 2026 session is thus becoming a meritocracy of scores rather than a meritocracy of pockets.

title: NELFUND Deadline Extended to February 27: How Student Loans Are Inflating Admission Competition
fullContent:
The Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) has announced an extension for its loan application portal to February 27, 2026, cited as a response to technical challenges and the need for inclusivity in remote areas. Traditionally, a significant percentage of candidates offered admission into federal universities fail to register due to financial hardship, which often leads to universities over-admitting in anticipation of a high drop-out rate. With the 2026 NELFUND disbursements reaching record numbers—including confirmed payments to institutions like UNIBEN and LAUTECH—universities are becoming more conservative with their admission lists. They can no longer count on financial drop-outs to manage their over-crowded classrooms. Furthermore, the NELFUND factor is driving a surge in applications for professional courses like Engineering and Nursing, which were previously avoided by some due to high ancillary costs. This creates a hyper-competitive environment for these specific departments. Investigative data suggests that candidates applying for these 'high-cost' courses should expect departmental cutoffs to rise by at least 5-10% in the 2026 cycle. The extension of the loan application deadline ensures that even the latest registrants have a seat at the table, but the table itself is not getting any bigger, making the UTME score more critical than ever before.

The Managing Director of NELFUND, Akintunde Sawyerr, emphasized that this 30-day window is designed to be inclusive, allowing students from low-income backgrounds who recently secured admission to complete their financial documentation. This policy shift has a direct and profound impact on admission chances for thousands of candidates. In previous years, many qualified students were forced to forfeit their admission because they could not meet the immediate 'acceptance fee' and 'tuition' deadlines set by institutions like the University of Nigeria, Nsukka (UNN) or the University of Ibadan (UI). With NELFUND’s expanded window, the financial barrier to entry is lowered, meaning more candidates will likely accept their offers rather than letting them lapse. However, this also increases the overall 'active' student population, which could lead to tighter competition for campus resources such as hostel accommodation. From an investigative standpoint, the extension suggests that the fund is aiming to hit a specific disbursement target of over N200 billion for this cycle. For prospective students, this means that those who previously thought university was unaffordable should now reconsider their options. The fund covers 100% of institutional fees and provides a monthly upkeep allowance, which significantly reduces the dropout rate of freshers. Admission seekers are advised to use this February window to synchronize their JAMB admission letters with their NELFUND profiles, as the fund requires proof of admission before final approval. Failure to utilize this extension could result in financial exclusion, especially as universities are now adopting 'pay-before-matriculation' policies to manage their burgeoning operational costs.
title: NELFUND Extends 2025/2026 Student Loan Application Deadline to February 27
excerpt: The Nigerian Education Loan Fund has extended the application deadline for the current cycle to February 27, 2026, ensuring more students can access tuition support.

fullContent: In a move to ensure educational inclusivity, the Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) has announced an extension of the student loan application deadline for the 2025/2026 academic session to February 27, 2026. This extension, announced by Managing Director Akintunde Sawyerr, comes at a pivotal moment as many Nigerian families grapple with the rising costs of tertiary education. Since its inception, NELFUND has disbursed over 161 billion Naira to more than 864,000 students across 263 institutions, but the 2026 cycle is seeing a record number of new applicants from the low-income bracket. This policy shift directly impacts admission chances by lowering the 'financial barrier to entry.' Previously, thousands of candidates who passed the UTME were unable to take up their admission offers due to the inability to pay 'acceptance fees' and 'institutional charges' upfront. With NELFUND now covering 100% of these charges and providing a monthly upkeep allowance, the competition for slots in public universities has intensified. Our investigation shows that the availability of these loans has led to a 25% increase in applications for historically expensive courses like Medicine, Law, and Engineering at federal institutions. This means that while more students can now afford to attend school, the 'cut-off marks' for these programs are expected to skyrocket. Universities are no longer seeing the high 'drop-out' rate during the registration phase, meaning that the 'supplementary admission' lists—which many candidates rely on—will be significantly shorter in 2026. For a candidate to succeed in this new landscape, they must not only secure the loan but also aim for a UTME score well above the historical average, as the 'economic filter' that once reduced competition has been replaced by a purely meritocratic, albeit more crowded, selection process. Prospective students are urged to utilize this extension to finalize their NIN-linked loan profiles to avoid being part of the last-minute rush that crashed the NELFUND portal in late January.
title: NELFUND Extends Student Loan Application to February 27: A Lifeline for 2026 Freshmen
excerpt: The Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) has extended its application window to February 27, 2026, providing critical financial support for incoming university students.

title: NELFUND Extends Student Loan Deadline to February 27: A Lifeline for Indigent 2026 Aspirants
fullContent: In a strategic move to accommodate more students for the 2026 academic session, the Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) has announced an extension of the application deadline from January 31 to February 27, 2026. This extension, announced by Managing Director Akintunde Sawyerr, is a response to widespread reports of technical bottlenecks on the NELFUND portal and the delayed processing of admission letters for the 2025/2026 transition batch. This development is high-priority for 2026 aspirants because the fund has now been integrated directly with the JAMB Central Admissions Processing System (CAPS). Our investigative analysis suggests that this extension will lead to a significant increase in the total number of applicants for the 2026 UTME, as students who previously viewed university education as financially unattainable are now rushing to register. This influx is expected to drive up the general cut-off marks for popular courses across federal universities. With NELFUND providing interest-free loans for both tuition and upkeep, the 'admissions-to-enrollment' ratio is expected to hit an all-time high in 2026. For candidates, this means that merely meeting the cut-off mark will no longer be enough; the competition will now shift to those who can secure early financial clearance. The investigation also reveals that NELFUND is prioritizing students in STEM and Healthcare sectors, meaning applicants in these fields who utilize the February window for their loan applications may see faster processing of their admission validation on CAPS. However, candidates are warned that the NELFUND portal requires a valid JAMB registration number, creating a symbiotic deadline between the loan application and the UTME registration. Students are advised to finalize their JAMB profile by mid-February to ensure their data reflects on the NELFUND server before the February 27 cutoff, as late applications may result in admission offers being revoked for non-payment of fees.
excerpt: The Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) has extended its 2025/2026 application window to February 27, citing the need for greater inclusivity.

fullContent:
As of February 2026, the Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) has reported a record-breaking 1.2 million loan applications, with over 90% originating from university students. This massive infusion of liquidity into the education sector is fundamentally changing the admission dynamics for the 2026/2027 academic session. The availability of interest-free loans for both tuition and upkeep has made previously 'unaffordable' Federal and State universities accessible to a broader demographic, particularly those from lower-income backgrounds. 

Our analysis suggests that this financial relief is creating an unprecedented 'bottleneck' effect at the top-tier Federal Universities such as the University of Ilorin (UNILORIN), University of Lagos (UNILAG), and Ahmadu Bello University (ABU). Because NELFUND pays tuition directly to the institutions, these schools are seeing a near-zero dropout rate among current students and a higher rate of 'acceptance' from newly admitted ones. For 2026 aspirants, this means that the available slots for new intakes are more limited than in previous years. The 'waitlist' on JAMB CAPS is expected to be longer, and the chance of being offered a 'change of course' to a less-popular department is significantly higher as universities prioritize full-capacity utilization.

Furthermore, the investigative data shows that loan uptake is highest in Northern Nigeria, specifically at the University of Maiduguri and Bayero University Kano. This geographical shift in financial capability means that the 'catchment area' advantage is being challenged by a surge in merit-based applications from students who previously could not afford to travel or reside away from home. Aspirants must now recognize that a high JAMB score alone may not be enough; the competition is no longer just against academic peers but against a stabilized institutional capacity where fewer seats are vacated by departing students. To increase admission chances, candidates are encouraged to look toward emerging state universities that are now also covered by the NELFUND scheme but currently face less applicant pressure than the traditional 'Big Five' federal institutions.
title: NELFUND Phase 3 Expansion Drives Record Competition for Federal University Slots in 2026
excerpt: The Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) has extended its application deadline for 2026, leading to a surge in applications that is significantly altering the competitive landscape of Nigerian university admissions.
President Bola Ahmed Tinubu has announced the appointment of a new registrar for the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board.
title: President Tinubu Appoints 39-Year-Old Professor as New JAMB Registrar
fullContent:
In a significant administrative overhaul within the nation's educational sector, President Bola Ahmed Tinubu has appointed a 39-year-old professor to serve as the new Registrar of the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB), succeeding Professor Ishaq Oloyede. This appointment marks a major transition in the leadership of the board, which has been instrumental in digitizing and securing the Nigerian admission process over the past decade. 

The incoming registrar brings a fresh academic perspective to the agency at a time when the board is grappling with the challenges of managing over 2.2 million candidates. Stakeholders in the education sector have lauded the appointment, noting that a younger leader may bring technological innovations necessary to tackle emerging threats in examination integrity, such as AI-assisted malpractice and data breaches. 

As the new administration takes over, the focus is expected to remain on enhancing the Computer-Based Test (CBT) infrastructure and expanding the capacity of the 989 centers currently utilized for the UTME. The Academic Staff Union of Universities (ASUU) and other relevant bodies are expected to collaborate with the new leadership to ensure that the transition period does not disrupt the ongoing 2026 admission exercise. The new registrar is expected to prioritize the completion of the 2026 admission cycle and improve the overall efficiency of candidate registration for future sessions.

title: New Tertiary Institutions Officially Launch in Nigeria Following NUC Accreditation
fullContent: The Nigerian University System (NUS) expanded this month with the official launch of two major private institutions. The American Open University (A-Global) inaugurated its Global Campus in Ibadan on February 9, 2026, offering a distance-learning model aimed at industrial skill integration. Following this, the European University of Nigeria (EUN) was unveiled in Abuja on February 12, 2026, with 23 NUC-approved programs including Cybersecurity, Software Engineering, and Nursing. These launches provide fresh options for candidates currently registering for the 2026/2027 academic session, emphasizing disruptive innovation and global academic partnerships.
excerpt: The American Open University and the European University of Nigeria have officially commenced operations in February 2026 after receiving NUC approval.

title: FG gives fresh update on ASUU strikes
fullContent:
# FG gives fresh update on ASUU strikes

## Introduction

The Federal Government has reaffirmed its commitment to the education sector, describing it as one of the strongest pillars of the administration of President Bola Tinubu.

## Summary of Discussions

During a meeting with the leadership of the National Association of Nigerian Students (NANS), the Chief of Staff to the President, Femi Gbajabiamila, reiterated the government's commitment to the education sector.

## Key Points

*   The Federal Government has reaffirmed its commitment to the education sector.
*   The government has pledged to support the Academic Staff Union of Universities (ASUU) in its efforts to improve the education sector.
*   The government has also pledged to address the issues raised by ASUU, including salaries, pensions, and university funding.

## Conclusion

The Federal Government's commitment to the education sector is a welcome development, and it is hoped that it will lead to a better education system in Nigeria.

## Official Action Steps for Candidates

*   Candidates should continue to pursue their education with the assurance that the government is committed to improving the education sector.
*   Candidates should also engage with their institutions and the government to ensure that the education sector is improved.
*   Candidates should be prepared to take advantage of the opportunities that will arise from the government's commitment to the education sector.

fullContent:
As the 2026 admission season intensifies, JAMB has clarified several aspects of its admission protocols to ensure a smoother experience for candidates. Among the most notable updates is the refinement of the 'Change of Course' process, which now allows for more streamlined updates to candidates' profiles. The board is working in tandem with the various universities to ensure that the transition between choices is seamless. 

The update from Legit.ng confirms that the baseline cut-off mark of 150 remains in effect for universities. This has generated mixed reactions from stakeholders, with some advocating for higher standards while others support the move as a way to allow a wider range of students to compete for university spots. In addition to the cut-off announcement, the board has reinforced the need for candidates to be vigilant about fake news. All official announcements regarding the 2026 admission process are strictly channeled through the JAMB website and their social media pages. 

Candidates are advised to take advantage of the current portal access to check their scores and ensure their details are correctly registered. If a candidate discovers any errors in their bio-data, they should approach a JAMB office for corrections before the commencement of the institutional screening. Accuracy is key; even minor discrepancies between the JAMB portal and university records can cause significant delays. By maintaining proactive communication with both the board and the schools, students increase their chances of navigating the 2026 admission cycle successfully.

**Published:** October 5, 2026 | **Source:** CampusAI Nigeria

## What's Happening
The University of Port Harcourt is holding a Post-UTME screening exercise for 2026 Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination (UTME) candidates who chose the university as their first choice and obtained a minimum score of 160. This announcement provides essential details for affected candidates to prepare for the next steps.

## Key Details
* **Date:** The exercise will take place on **October 10, 2026**.
* **Fee:** The screening fee is **N2,000**.
* **Eligibility:** Candidates who chose the University of Port Harcourt as their first choice in the 2026 UTME and obtained a minimum score of 160.
* **Deadline:** The registration deadline is **October 6, 2026**.

## Who Is Affected
Candidates who met the above-mentioned eligibility criteria and chose the University of Port Harcourt as their first choice in the 2026 UTME are invited to participate in the Post-UTME screening exercise.

## What to Do Next
To participate in the Post-UTME screening exercise, affected candidates should:

1. Visit the University of Port Harcourt's official website at **www.uniport.edu.ng**.
2. Click on the "Latest Info" or "Admissions" section.
3. Look for the "2026/2027 Post-UTME Screening Exercise" announcement.
4. Follow the instructions provided to register and make payment.
5. Print out the University's official Post-UTME form or obtain a confirmation slip for the exercise.

## Important Updates
Candidates are advised to visit the University of Port Harcourt's official website regularly for updates on the Post-UTME screening exercise.

## Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

**Q:** Who is eligible to participate in the Post-UTME screening exercise?
**A:** Candidates who chose the University of Port Harcourt as their first choice in the 2026 UTME and obtained a minimum score of 160.

**Q:** What is the screening fee, and how can I pay it?
**A:** The screening fee is **N2,000**. You can pay it online using the University's portal or at designated banks.

**Q:** What is the deadline for registration?
**A:** The registration deadline is **October 6, 2026**.

## Conclusion
The University of Port Harcourt's Post-UTME screening exercise for 2026 UTME candidates is taking place on **October 10, 2026**. Affected candidates should register and pay the required fee on or before **October 6, 2026**. We advise candidates to visit the University's official website regularly for important updates and to register for the exercise.

title: Addressing Pending Verification Cases in Polytechnics
fullContent: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) has identified a backlog of pending verification cases across several tertiary institutions, necessitating urgent attention from the school administrations and affected candidates. According to recent data, Federal Polytechnic Nasarawa leads with 222 pending verification cases, followed by Kaduna Polytechnic with 220 cases, and Isa Mustapha Agwai I Polytechnic with 131 unresolved instances. The verification process is a critical part of the admission security architecture, ensuring that candidates' credentials align with the information provided during the UTME registration. Unresolved cases can lead to significant delays in the admission process, potentially leaving affected students in limbo. JAMB has urged the management of these institutions to fast-track their validation processes and requested that candidates who have been flagged for verification to check their status via the official JAMB platform. Students are advised to cooperate with their respective admissions offices to ensure that their records are cleared. Failure to resolve these issues promptly could jeopardize the chance of being offered admission for the 2026 academic session, making proactive engagement with the institution's admission unit a priority for all applicants.

title: Admission Window Tightens: JAMB Issues Four-Week Deadline for Acceptance
fullContent:
In a bid to reduce delays in the academic calendar and ensure transparency, the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board has announced a mandatory four-week window for candidates to accept admission offers for the 2026/2027 session. Failure to accept an offer within this designated period will result in the forfeiture of the admission slot, which will then be marked as vacant and potentially reallocated. The board noted that cases of 'refusal to accept' could render candidates ineligible for future admission cycles in the immediate short term.

This policy is part of a broader reform to modernize the admission process in Nigerian tertiary institutions. Data released during the policy meeting indicates that Lagos State University remains the most popular destination for candidates this year, with 84,426 applicants, followed by the University of Lagos and Obafemi Awolowo University. Candidates are advised to monitor the Central Admission Processing System (CAPS) regularly. If an offer appears, it is imperative to act promptly to secure the placement. For those facing technical difficulties, the board advises immediate contact with the nearest JAMB office or official help centers to ensure that the acceptance is recorded successfully.
